title: The Wounded Cub in the Bamboo Grove
content:
The bamboo groaned against the wind, a sound like old men complaining about their joints. Su Yun pressed his back against the splintered stalk and held his breath. The boar — a Zhu with tusks longer than his forearm — had stopped ten paces away. Its snout twitched, sucking in the scent of morning rain, crushed leaves, and the boy who had wandered too far from the village.

Su Yun's hand closed around the rope coiled at his belt. Not much of a weapon. But it was all he had.

The Zhu snorted, then turned and crashed away through the underbrush. Su Yun let out the breath he'd been holding. Stupid. He knew better than to hunt this deep into the bamboo groves. The forest didn't belong to men. It belonged to the beasts. Everyone in Green Valley Village understood this. Everyone who wanted to keep living, anyway.

He should head back. Grandmother would have the morning rice ready, and if he was late again, she'd hit him with the ladle and call him a useless mouth to feed. He could already hear her voice, cracked and sharp as dried clay.

But something stopped him. A sound beneath the wind. Small. High. Like a kitten, maybe. Or a bird with a broken wing.

Su Yun's feet moved before his head could argue. He pushed through the bamboo, following the sound deeper into the grove where the light turned green and watery. The air grew cool against his skin. The smell of crushed stalks gave way to something else. Copper. Blood.

He found it in a tangle of fallen bamboo, half-buried under leaves that had turned black with old rain.

A cub.

At first, he thought it was dead. It lay on its side, ribs rising and falling in quick, shallow movements. The fur was the color of ash mixed with blood, matted against a body that should have been bigger. Su Yun crouched, his rope forgotten in his hands.

The cub opened its eyes.

They were white. Pure, pale white, like milk. No iris. No pupil. Just white, staring at him from a face that was half-cat, half-something else. The cub bared its teeth — fangs, tiny but sharp — and tried to hiss. The sound came out wet and broken.

Su Yun's heart stopped.

He knew what this was. Every child in the Nine Realms grew up hearing the stories. The Qiongqi. The winged tiger from the ancient days, written in the Shan Hai Jing, bound to chaos and destruction. The beast that could not be tamed.

This one was dying.

A gash ran along its side, deep enough to see the white gleam of bone. Its front leg bent at an angle that made Su Yun's stomach turn. One of its wings — small, useless-looking things on a cub this young — hung twisted and torn.

Su Yun should leave. He knew this. A Qiongqi cub, even half-dead, meant trouble. If the sect found out he'd touched it, they'd call it theft. They'd call it treason. They'd kill him, maybe. Or worse — they'd take him to the mountain and make an example of him for the other villagers to see.

But the cub looked at him with those white eyes, and it stopped struggling. It just lay there, watching him, waiting.

Waiting to die, maybe. Or waiting for something else.

Su Yun reached out. His hand shook. The rope hung loose from his other hand, dragging in the dirt.

"You're going to kill me," he whispered. "You know that, right?"

The cub didn't answer. It just kept watching.

Su Yun picked it up.

The cub weighed nothing. Bones and wet fur and a heartbeat that fluttered like a trapped moth. He wrapped it in his jacket, the only jacket he owned, and carried it out of the bamboo grove. The Zhu didn't come back. The forest stayed quiet, as if it was watching too, waiting to see what the stable boy would do with a piece of chaos in his arms.

---

Grandmother was waiting at the door.

She took one look at the bundle in his arms, and her face went the color of old ash. "What did you bring home?"

"Nothing," Su Yun said. "Just a cat."

Her eyes narrowed. She'd raised him since his mother died and his father walked into the forest and never came back. She knew every lie he'd ever told. "That's not a cat."

"It's just a cat."

"Su Yun."

"It was dying, Grandmother."

She stared at him for a long moment. Then she stepped aside. "Put it in the shed. If the sect finds out—"

"They won't."

"You don't know that."

"I know," Su Yun said, and he believed it. He had to believe it.

The shed was small, dark, smelled of hay and dried dung. He laid the cub on a pile of old blankets, the only soft thing he owned that wasn't his jacket. The cub's eyes had closed. For a terrible moment, Su Yun thought it had died in his arms. But the ribs still moved. The heartbeat still fluttered.

He cleaned the wound with water and a rag that wasn't too dirty. The cub didn't stir. He set the leg as best he could, using sticks from the yard and strips torn from his shirt. The cub whimpered once, a sound that cut through Su Yun's chest like a blade, and then went still again.

He sat beside it until the light through the cracks in the wall turned orange. His grandmother called him for dinner. He didn't go.

"They'll kill it," he said to the cub. "If they find you, they'll kill you. And they'll kill me too."

The cub opened its eyes. Those white eyes, depthless and strange, fixed on his face.

"Why are you looking at me like that?" Su Yun asked. "Do you even understand what I'm saying?"

The cub blinked. Slowly. Deliberately. Like it understood everything.

"Yeah," Su Yun said, and he laughed, a broken sound. "You probably do. You're a Qiongqi. You probably understand more than I do."

He looked down at the rope still coiled at his belt. It was just a rope. A piece of hemp twisted by hands older than his, used for tying firewood and hauling water. But beast tamers used ropes too. The cultivators from the sect, the ones who walked through the village with their heads high and their beasts on leashes made of silk and gold — they used ropes to bind their creatures.

Su Yun picked up the rope. It felt heavy in his hands.

He didn't know the rituals. He didn't know the words. He was a stable boy, not a cultivator. He mucked out stalls and fed horses and dreamed of things he'd never have.

But he knew a bond when he felt one.

He looped the rope around the cub's neck. It was too big. It hung loose, sliding down over the matted fur. But the cub didn't fight. It just watched him.

"I don't know if this means anything," Su Yun said. "I don't know what I'm doing. But I'm not going to let them kill you."

The cub's tail twitched. Once.

And somewhere deep in Su Yun's chest, something stirred. A warmth. A pulse. A thread of connection, thin as spider silk, stretching from his heart to the heart of the little beast beside him.

He didn't know what it meant.

But he felt it.

---

The announcement came three days later.

Su Yun was in the stable, forking hay into the horses' troughs, when the gong sounded from the village square. The sound rolled through the morning air like thunder, heavy and insistent. The horses stamped and nickered. Su Yun's hands went still on the pitchfork.

He knew that gong. Everyone knew that gong. It only sounded for one thing.

The sect had come.

He dropped the pitchfork and ran.

The square was already full. Villagers in their work clothes, farmers with dirt under their nails, women with children on their hips — they all stood in a loose circle around the platform that had been erected overnight. Su Yun pushed to the front, ignoring the elbows and muttered curses.

On the platform stood a man in blue robes. The color of the Azure Cloud Sect. His face was young but his eyes were old, and he carried himself like someone who had never been told no in his life. Behind him stood two beasts. One was a Bi Fang — a bird with a single leg and a crest of red feathers that burned like fire. The other was a Zhu Yan, a creature with the body of a bull and the head of a man, its teeth bared in a grin that made Su Yun's skin crawl.

The man raised his hand. The crowd fell silent.

"The Nine Realms Tournament," he said, and his voice carried without effort, smooth and practiced, "begins in three moons. The Azure Cloud Sect invites all who wish to compete. The prize: one hundred thousand spirit stones, a place among the sect elders, and the right to claim any beast in the Nine Realms."

Murmurs rippled through the crowd. One hundred thousand spirit stones. A lifetime of wealth. More than Green Valley Village would see in a hundred years.

Su Yun's hands clenched at his sides.

"Registration is open to all," the man continued. "No entry fee. No restrictions. Bring your beast. Prove your worth. Or die trying."

He smiled. It was not a kind smile.

Su Yun turned and walked away before the crowd could disperse. He went back to the shed, where the Qiongqi cub was curled on the blankets, its wound healing pink and new, its leg straight again. It opened its white eyes when he entered.

"The tournament," Su Yun said. "You heard about it?"

The cub tilted its head.

"I'm going to enter."

The cub blinked.

"I know. I'm a stable boy with a rope and a half-starved cat. But they're going to take our village's harvest this year. They're going to take everything. And if I don't do something—" He stopped. Swallowed. "I have to try."

The cub stood. Slowly, carefully, it crossed the shed and pressed its head against his hand.

Su Yun looked down at the rope still tied around its neck.

"You're not much," he said. "And I'm not much either. But maybe that's enough."

The cub purred. A sound like rocks grinding together, deep and warm.

Su Yun smiled.

It was a small smile. Uncertain. Afraid.

But it was a start.

title: The Tiger That Wouldn't Eat
content:
The Qiongqi cub hadn't moved in three hours.

Su Yun sat cross-legged in the stables, straw prickling through his trousers, watching the creature press itself against the far wall. Its ribs showed through matted fur. One wing hung at an angle that made his gut twist — broken, probably. The other wing twitched every few breaths, scattering dust motes in the lamplight.

"You have to eat something," he said.

The cub's eyes tracked him. Golden irises with black slits. No fear in them. Something worse — exhaustion so deep it looked like acceptance.

Su Yun pushed a bowl of shredded meat across the floor. "I know you're a predator. The texts say you eat people. But this is goat. Fresh. I killed it myself this morning."

Nothing.

He'd found the cub three days ago, tangled in a hunting net outside the village walls. The net belonged to the Scarlet Feather Sect — he knew because their emblem was stamped into the rope, a crimson bird with three claws. They'd probably caught the cub for the tournament. Probably lost it during transport.

Their loss was going to get him killed.

The village elders hadn't said much when he'd dragged the cub through the gates. They'd just looked at each other with that particular expression farmers wore when they saw a wolf among their sheep. Old Chen had spat tobacco juice and muttered something about bad omens.

Su Yun didn't believe in omens. He believed in the scar across his ribs from last winter, when he'd fought a starving bear for a deer carcass and lost. He believed in the empty grain stores and the collection notices from the Scarlet Feather Sect nailed to every door.

"We have a problem," said a voice behind him.

Su Yun didn't turn. He knew that voice. "What kind of problem, Mayor?"

Mayor Huang stepped into the lamplight, his silk robes rustling. The man was fifty, with a face like cracked leather and eyes that had seen too many bad harvests. He held a scroll in one hand, the red seal already broken.

"The tournament registration closes in seven days. The Scarlet Feather Sect has officially entered three tamers. They're sending them tomorrow to collect their beast."

Su Yun's stomach dropped. "This one's mine. I found it. I fed it."

"You bonded with it?" The mayor's voice was flat. He knew the answer.

Su Yun looked at the cub. The ritual of bonding required blood, breath, and intent. He'd done all three, desperately, before he'd even cut the net away. The silver mark on his palm — a circle with two crossed lines — was still fresh enough to sting.

"I bonded with it."

"Then you're a beast tamer." The mayor said it like he was pronouncing a death sentence. "Which means you have to enter the tournament. Village law. You know this."

Su Yun knew. Every village in the Nine Realms had the same law — any bonded tamer must compete in the tournament or forfeit their land. The tournament was how the Scarlet Feather Sect controlled the outer villages. Send a few desperate farmers into the arena. Watch them die. Collect the territory when the families couldn't pay the entry fees.

"The cub can barely stand," Su Yun said. "It won't survive the journey to the capital, let alone the tournament."

"Then you should have thought of that before you bonded with a Qiongqi."

Something in the cub's eyes flickered. Su Yun could have sworn it understood.

"If I could find it food—"

"The sect is coming tomorrow." The mayor cut him off, rolling the scroll back up. "They'll bring the tournament master. You'll be assessed. If your beast is deemed unfit, they'll confiscate it and you'll be fined for breaking the bonding laws."

"The fine is—"

"Five thousand silver." The mayor's voice softened, just slightly. "You don't have it, Su Yun. No one in this village does."

He left, his footsteps crunching away through the frozen mud.

Su Yun sat in the silence, staring at the cub. It stared back. Neither of them blinked.

"The Qiongqi is a devourer of hope," he said quietly, quoting from the Shan Hai Jing that his mother had taught him before she died. "It eats the dreams of men and shits out despair."

The cub's ear twitched.

"But you're not eating anything, are you?" Su Yun leaned forward. "You're starving. You're broken. And I have seven days to make you fight."

He reached out his hand, palm up. The silver mark glowed faintly.

The cub's nostrils flared. It sniffed the air, once, twice. Then it moved.

Not fast. Not aggressive. It crawled across the stable floor like a cat that had been beaten too many times, dragging its broken wing behind it. When it reached his hand, it pressed its forehead against his palm.

The bond flared to life.

Su Yun gasped. Images flooded his mind — a mountain peak covered in red snow, a sky full of wings, a voice that wasn't words but hunger. Not hunger for food. Hunger for purpose. For belonging. For something the cub had never had.

He pulled his hand back, breathing hard.

The cub looked at him. For the first time, there was something other than exhaustion in its eyes.

Curiosity.

"Okay," Su Yun said. "Okay. I have an idea."

---

The Scarlet Feather Sect arrived at dawn.

Three tamers on horseback, their beasts walking beside them. Su Yun watched from the stable door, counting. The first tamer had a Zhuque — a red bird with a snake's tail and scales like molten copper. The second had a Yingzhao, a horse with a human face and hooves that left scorch marks in the mud. The third was their leader, a man named Sect Elder Gu, and he rode a Bai Ze.

The Bai Ze was a lion with two horns and a human face. It could speak. It knew the names of all demons and spirits, and it had never lost a fight in the Nine Realms.

Su Yun's hands were shaking. He pressed them flat against his thighs.

The village had gathered in the square. Fifty faces, all of them familiar, all of them watching him with that same expression — pity mixed with the desperate hope that he might somehow save them.

Sect Elder Gu dismounted. He was tall, with a shaved head and a scar that split his left eyebrow in two. His robes were red and gold, the sect's emblem embroidered across his chest.

"Su Yun," he said. Not a question.

"Yes, Elder."

"I heard you bonded with a Qiongqi." The man's smile was thin. "I also heard it's a cub. Half-dead. Worthless."

Su Yun said nothing.

"Bring it out."

He turned and walked into the stable. The cub was awake, watching him with those golden eyes. He'd fed it the goat meat last night, after he'd figured out the trick — it wouldn't eat from a bowl, but it would eat from his hand. It had bitten him twice before it understood.

"Come on," he said. "Time to meet the crowd."

The cub stood. Slowly. Painfully. Its broken wing dragged on the ground.

Together, they walked out into the morning light.

The crowd gasped. The sect tamers laughed. One of them — the one with the Zhuque — pointed and said something Su Yun couldn't hear.

Sect Elder Gu's smile widened. "That's your bonded beast? It can barely walk."

"It can fight."

"It can die."

Su Yun met his eyes. "All things die, Elder. The question is how much damage they do before they fall."

The Bai Ze spoke. Its voice was like stones grinding together. "This one has spirit. It would be a shame to waste it in the arena."

Elder Gu glanced at his beast. "You sense something?"

"The bond is strong. Stronger than it should be." The Bai Ze's human face turned toward Su Yun, studying him. "He did something. I cannot tell what."

Su Yun kept his expression blank. Last night, after the cub had eaten, he'd done something no tamer was supposed to do. He'd shared his own energy — his life force, the thing cultivators called qi — through the bond. It was dangerous. It was stupid. It had nearly killed him.

But the cub had healed. Just a little. Just enough to stand.

"We'll assess the beast in the arena," Elder Gu said. "If it can defeat one of our training beasts, it's fit for the tournament. If not, you forfeit your bond and pay the fine."

The training beast was a Gouchen. A dog with a dragon's head and iron scales. It was three times the size of the Qiongqi cub and had been bred for exactly one purpose — testing the worth of new tamers.

Su Yun had seen a Gouchen kill a man last year. It had taken less than ten seconds.

"Bring it out," Elder Gu said.

The tamer with the Yingzhao walked to the cart they'd brought. He lifted a cage covered in black cloth. The thing inside shifted, chains rattling.

Su Yun knelt beside the cub. "I need you to trust me."

The cub's eyes met his.

"I know you're tired. I know you're scared. But I'm scared too, and I'm still standing." He put his hand on its head. "We do this together, or we don't do it at all."

The cub's tail twitched. Once.

Then it walked toward the cage.

The crowd fell silent. The Gouchen was released — a mass of iron scales and teeth, its dragon head snapping at the air. It saw the cub and lunged.

The cub didn't fight.

It opened its mouth and made a sound that wasn't a roar.

It was a song.

Low and mournful, like wind through dead trees, the sound washed over the Gouchen. The beast stopped mid-lunge. Its eyes went glassy. It sat down, head cocked, listening.

The crowd stared.

Sect Elder Gu's face went pale.

The Qiongqi cub sang for thirty seconds. When it stopped, the Gouchen was asleep, snoring like a drunkard.

Su Yun looked at Elder Gu. "Is that fit enough for you?"

The silence stretched.

Then the Bai Ze laughed. It was a horrible sound, like breaking bones. "Oh, this tournament just got interesting."

Elder Gu's jaw tightened. "The beast is... acceptable. You will report to the capital in six days. Do not be late."

He turned and walked away, his tamers scrambling after him.

The village erupted in cheers.

Su Yun didn't cheer. He was watching the cub, which had collapsed the moment the Gouchen fell asleep. Its breathing was shallow. Its eyes were closed.

He carried it back to the stable and laid it in the straw.

"You used all your energy on that song, didn't you?"

The cub didn't answer.

"Stupid tiger. Wasting yourself on a show."

He sat beside it, exhaustion pulling at his bones. Six days. He had six days to get a half-dead cub ready for the deadliest tournament in the Nine Realms.

Six days to teach a beast that didn't know how to fight.

He closed his eyes.

The cub's tail curled around his wrist.

And somewhere, deep in the Shan Hai mountain range, an ancient creature woke from its thousand-year sleep, drawn by the sound of a song it hadn't heard since the world was young.

title: The Blood Price of Bonding
content:
The tournament grounds sprawled across three valleys, connected by stone bridges that arched over mist-filled chasms. Su Yun had never seen so many people in one place. Thousands of cultivators crowded the registration platforms, their beasts tethered to bronze posts or coiled around their shoulders like living jewelry.

A woman walked past with a Zouwu draped across her neck—a five-tailed leopard that purred like grinding stones. A man in silk robes carried a juvenile Bifang on his shoulder, the single-legged crane eyeing the crowd with predatory disinterest. Each beast radiated power that made Su Yun’s skin prickle.

He clutched the rope tied around Qiongqi’s neck. The cub had stopped shivering, but its bones still showed through patchy fur. A woman in orange robes pointed at them and laughed. Her companion joined in.

“Did he bring a wild dog?”

“Look at it. That thing’s going to die in the qualifiers.”

Su Yun kept walking. His fingers tightened on the rope until his knuckles went white.

The registration desk stretched across a hundred feet of carved jade. Twenty officials sat behind it, each processing a different tier of competitor. Su Yun found the one marked “Unranked” and joined the shortest line.

An hour passed. Then two.

Qiongqi sat at his feet, watching the other beasts with unnerving stillness. Su Yun had expected it to cower. Instead, the cub studied each creature like a hunter memorizing prey. Its golden eyes tracked every movement, every flick of a tail, every bared fang.

“Next.”

The official didn’t look up from his scroll. He was old, with white hair pulled into a topknot and cultivation robes so plain they might have been peasant clothes. But his hands moved with precision that spoke of decades of practice.

“Name?”

“Su Yun of Crescent Village.”

“Beast?”

“Qiongqi.”

The official’s brush stopped. He looked up for the first time, his eyes scanning the cub with professional assessment. “A Qiongqi. That’s a bold choice for an unranked tamer.”

“I didn’t choose it. It chose me.”

“They always say that.” The official dipped his brush. “You know the rules?”

“No.”

This earned a thin smile. “At least you’re honest. First qualifier is tomorrow at dawn. You’ll be assigned a hunting zone in the Eastern Valleys. Capture a beast of at least Silver rank within three hours, or you’re eliminated.”

“Capture it alive?”

“Capture it however you can. But the beast must survive to be registered. Dead beasts disqualify you.”

Su Yun nodded. He’d never hunted a Silver rank beast. The strongest thing he’d ever faced was a corrupted Iron-rank hare that had been terrorizing his village’s cabbage fields.

“One more thing.” The official leaned forward, his voice dropping. “The tournament has a blood price. Every year, some tamers don’t come back. The beasts here are not civilized. They are not trained pets. They are the children of Shan Hai Jing, and they remember when humans were prey.”

The official handed him a copper token stamped with the character for “Seven.” “Zone Seven. Don’t be late.”

---

Su Yun found a spot against the outer wall of the competitor’s compound. He’d spent his last three copper coins on dried meat and water, and now he sat with his back to cold stone, sharing half his meal with Qiongqi.

The cub ate like it hadn’t seen food in weeks. Which it hadn’t.

“We need a plan,” Su Yun said, tearing a strip of meat with his teeth. “I don’t know anything about Silver rank beasts. I don’t know anything about hunting. I don’t—”

A shadow fell over him.

He looked up to find a young man in forest-green robes, a juvenile Xiezhi at his side—a goat-like beast with a single horn and scales instead of fur. The beast’s silver eyes held an intelligence that made Su Yun’s skin crawl.

“You’re the one with the Qiongqi.”

It wasn’t a question. Su Yun stood slowly, keeping his body between the stranger and his cub.

“I am.”

“Name’s Lin Feng. Third rank disciple of the Heavenly Harmony Sect.” The young man smiled, but it didn’t reach his eyes. “I wanted to see the fool who thought he could tame a Qiongqi with a piece of rope.”

Qiongqi growled. The sound came from deep in its chest, barely audible but felt in the bones.

“He’s not a thing to be tamed,” Su Yun said. “He’s my partner.”

Lin Feng laughed. The Xiezhi sniffed the air, its nostrils flaring. “Partner. How quaint. Do you know what happened to the last person who tried to bond with a Qiongqi?”

“Tell me.”

“He died. The beast ate his heart while he was still breathing. They found them three days later—the Qiongqi was curled around his corpse, protecting it from scavengers.” Lin Feng’s smile widened. “Qiongqi are death spirits. They don’t bond. They wait.”

Su Yun felt the words like physical blows. He glanced down at the cub, at its ribs showing through patchy fur, at the way it pressed against his leg as if seeking warmth.

“You’re wrong.”

“Am I? Check your chest, stable boy. Do you have a mark?”

Su Yun’s hand went to his sternum. Beneath his shirt, his fingers found raised flesh—a pattern he’d assumed was a bruise from the fall. But now, touching it, he felt heat. A pulse that matched his heartbeat.

“You have one, don’t you?” Lin Feng’s voice turned cold. “The blood mark. It means the bond is incomplete. The beast hasn’t decided whether to accept you or eat you. The tournament will force that decision.”

He turned to leave, the Xiezhi following at his heel. “I hope you survive, stable boy. It would be a waste to die before the interesting matches.”

---

Su Yun didn’t sleep that night.

He sat in the corner of the compound, Qiongqi curled in his lap, and watched the stars wheel overhead. The cub’s warmth was the only thing that felt real. Everything else—the tournament, the cultivators, Lin Feng’s words—seemed like a dream he couldn’t wake from.

“I don’t know if he was telling the truth,” Su Yun whispered. “But I know one thing. I’m not giving up on you.”

Qiongqi’s ear twitched.

“We’re going to survive tomorrow. We’re going to capture a Silver rank beast. And then we’re going to win this tournament. Not because I’m strong. Because I have nowhere else to go.”

The cub lifted its head. Its golden eyes met his, and for a moment, Su Yun felt something pass between them—a spark of recognition, of understanding.

Then Qiongqi yawned, showing needle-sharp teeth, and went back to sleep.

---

Dawn came cold and grey.

Su Yun reported to Zone Seven with a hundred other competitors. The Eastern Valleys stretched before them, a maze of gorges and caves, thick with mist that swallowed sound and light.

The tournament officials gave brief instructions: stay within the zone boundaries, do not attack other competitors, survive until the bell rings.

Su Yun tightened the rope around Qiongqi’s neck. The cub was alert now, ears pricked forward, muscles tense beneath its patchy fur.

“Ready?” Su Yun asked.

Qiongqi growled. It sounded like agreement.

The bell rang.

Beast tamers surged forward, their creatures howling and screeching, fanning out into the mist. Su Yun held back, watching the chaos unfold around him. He’d learned long ago that charging into danger was a good way to die.

“We go slow,” he told Qiongqi. “We watch. We wait for an opening.”

The cub flicked its tail. Whether it understood or not, it stayed close as Su Yun crept into the valley, following the edge of a cliff where the mist was thinner.

An hour passed. Then two.

He saw tamers fighting beasts, beasts fighting each other, tamers fighting tamers over claiming rights. He saw a man get thrown off a cliff by a rampaging Baize. He saw a woman’s Xun—a six-tailed fox—biting through the spine of a Lesser Yaoguai.

But he didn’t find anything he could capture.

“We’re running out of time,” Su Yun muttered, checking the position of the sun. Two hours left. Maybe less.

Qiongqi suddenly stopped. Its ears flattened. A low rumble built in its chest.

“What is it?”

The cub didn’t answer. It stared into a cave entrance half-hidden by hanging vines. The darkness within seemed to breathe.

Su Yun’s instincts screamed at him to run.

But he had nothing else. No plan, no backup, no other options.

He stepped into the cave.

The smell hit him first—copper and rot, the stench of fresh blood. His eyes adjusted slowly to the dim light filtering through cracks in the ceiling.

And then he saw it.

A beast the size of a horse, covered in scales that gleamed like polished iron. Its head was feline, but its body was serpentine, with six legs ending in claws that had carved trenches into the stone floor. Its eyes were pits of molten gold.

Su Yun recognized it from the shan hai jing illustrations in his grandmother’s book.

A Zhulong.

A Silver-rank beast that breathed shadows instead of fire. A creature that could swallow light and leave its prey blind before it struck.

The Zhulong turned its head. It had been feeding on something—a corpse that Su Yun couldn’t identify, too torn apart to recognize. Blood dripped from its jaws.

It focused on him.

On Qiongqi.

The cub didn’t flinch. Didn’t retreat. Instead, it stepped forward, placing itself between Su Yun and the predator.

And it growled.

Not the weak, hungry sound from before. This was something else. Something ancient. A vibration that resonated in Su Yun’s chest, that made the air tremble.

The Zhulong hesitated.

For a split second, the larger beast looked uncertain. Its golden eyes flickered between the cub and the shadows gathering around its own body.

Then it lunged.

Su Yun barely had time to throw himself sideways. The Zhulong’s claws raked the air where he’d been standing, carving furrows in the stone floor. He hit the ground hard, scrambling backward, reaching for Qiongqi—

The cub was already moving.

Small and fast, it darted under the Zhulong’s strike, sinking its teeth into the larger beast’s foreleg. The Zhulong roared, thrashing, trying to dislodge the cub. But Qiongqi held on, jaws locked, claws digging into scales.

Blood sprayed.

Su Yun grabbed a rock and threw it. It bounced off the Zhulong’s head, doing nothing. He threw another. And another. Anything to distract it.

The Zhulong turned. Its molten eyes fixed on him.

And behind it, Qiongqi climbed.

The cub scrambled up the Zhulong’s back, using its scales as handholds, ignoring the thrashing and the roaring. It reached the base of the beast’s neck and bit down.

Deep.

The Zhulong screamed. Not a roar—a scream, high-pitched and desperate. It collapsed, legs folding, body going still.

Su Yun

title: The First Blood and the Last Laugh
content:
The morning fog clung to Su Yun's robes like a second skin.

Three days had passed since the tournament began. Three days of watching stronger contestants parade past his camp with captured beasts—xiao yaoguai that chittered in bamboo cages, feathered serpents coiled around their masters' arms. Three days of eating cold rice and watching Qiongqi grow weaker.

The cub hadn't eaten in two days.

Su Yun sat cross-legged on the damp grass, the Shan Hai Jing scroll spread across his knees. The ancient text spoke of Qiongqi's nature—"a beast that feeds on conflict, that grows strong on the blood of battle, that bonds only with those who have stared into the abyss and refused to blink."

He looked at the cub. It looked back with those mismatched eyes—one gold, one silver—and yawned, revealing needle-sharp fangs.

"You're not helping," Su Yun muttered.

Something rustled in the bamboo grove to his left.

He was on his feet before his mind caught up, the rope coiled in his hand. The cub tensed beside him, a low growl building in its throat.

"Easy," he whispered. "We don't know what it is."

The bamboo parted.

A boy stepped out—maybe fourteen, maybe younger, with the kind of soft hands that had never held a rope before. His robes were silk, embroidered with the golden crane of the Celestial Harmony Sect. Behind him, a crimson serpent slithered, its scales catching the morning light like polished rubies.

"Found you," the boy said, smiling. "I'm Lin Wei. Third disciple of Master Zhao." He tilted his head. "You're the stable boy, right? The one with the crippled beast?"

Su Yun's jaw tightened. "She's not crippled."

"She's starving." Lin Wei's smile widened. "I've been watching you. Three days, and you haven't caught a single creature. You haven't even tried." He stepped closer, the serpent rising behind him. "The tournament only lasts a month. At this rate, you'll be eliminated before the first week ends."

Su Yun said nothing. His hand found the rope's end, felt its familiar weight.

"I'm offering you a deal," Lin Wei continued. "Give me that Qiongqi. It's useless to you anyway. But to the Celestial Harmony Sect—well, we have resources. We can find a use for it. In exchange, I'll give you a lesser beast. Something you can actually bond with." He snapped his fingers, and the serpent hissed. "Maybe even a Jiao. They're common, but they can carry you through the first round."

The cub pressed against Su Yun's leg. He could feel it trembling—not from fear, but from hunger. From rage.

"No."

Lin Wei's smile flickered. "I'm trying to help you."

"I didn't ask for help."

"You're embarrassing yourself." The boy's voice sharpened. "Everyone's talking about it. The stable boy who thinks he's a tamer. The cripple with the crippled beast." He gestured at the cub. "That thing should have been put down at birth. It's an aberration. A mistake."

Something cold settled in Su Yun's chest.

"You don't know anything about her."

"I know she's going to die." Lin Wei's voice was flat now. "Maybe today. Maybe tomorrow. But she's going to die, and you're going to watch, and then you're going to go back to your village and spend the rest of your life feeding horses." He turned away, the serpent following. "Think about my offer. It won't stand forever."

He walked into the bamboo, and the forest swallowed him whole.

Su Yun stood there for a long moment, breathing hard. The cub whined, nudging his hand.

"I know," he said quietly. "I know."

---

That night, Su Yun made a decision.

The tournament grounds stretched for miles—a patchwork of forests, rivers, and mountains, each zone seeded with beasts from the Shan Hai Jing. The deeper you went, the more dangerous they became. And the more valuable.

Most contestants stayed in the outer zones, capturing lesser beasts for points. But the real prizes—the legendary beasts that could change a tamer's fate—lived in the heart of the tournament grounds. In the place they called the Crimson Valley.

No one had ever come back from the Crimson Valley.

Su Yun packed his rope, his scroll, and his empty rice pouch. The cub watched him with those mismatched eyes, a question in their depths.

"We're going in," he told her. "Deep."

She tilted her head.

"I know it's dangerous. I know we might not make it." He knelt, meeting her gaze. "But we're going to die out here anyway. Either from hunger, or from someone like Lin Wei, or from the shame of going home with nothing." He touched her head, felt the warmth of her fur. "I'd rather die trying."

The cub licked his hand. Then she stood, tail high, and walked toward the treeline.

She stopped. Looked back.

Waiting for him.

Su Yun laughed—a broken, desperate sound—and followed.

---

They walked through the night.

The outer zones gave way to denser forest, where the trees grew so thick that moonlight barely touched the ground. Strange sounds echoed through the darkness—calls that might have been birds, might have been something else. The cub moved ahead of him, her nose to the ground, her ears swiveling at every sound.

She was hunting.

Su Yun followed, trusting her instincts. She was a Qiongqi. Even starving, even weak, she was still a Qiongqi. And Qiongqi were apex predators.

At dawn, she found something.

A clearing, bathed in pale light. In the center, a pool of water so clear it looked like glass. And drinking from it, a creature Su Yun had only seen in the scrolls.

The Xiāo was beautiful in the way a knife was beautiful. Its body was like a snow leopard, but larger, its fur white with black rosettes. Its face was human-like—almost—with intelligent eyes that watched them both with cold calculation. A single horn curved from its forehead, spiraling like a unicorn's.

It was a fourth-rank beast. Su Yun knew that from Master Han's lessons. Fourth-rank meant it could kill a dozen armed men without breaking a sweat.

The Xiāo lifted its head, water dripping from its muzzle.

It looked at the cub. The cub looked back.

For a long moment, nothing moved.

Then the Xiāo spoke, its voice like wind through bamboo. "You bring a starving thing to my pool. Is this a challenge?"

Su Yun's blood went cold.

The cub stepped forward. She didn't growl. Didn't bare her teeth. She just walked toward the Xiāo, her gait steady, her eyes locked on its face.

"You are bold," the Xiāo said. "Or foolish."

The cub kept walking.

"The difference is often small."

Ten feet away, the cub stopped. She sat down. And she waited.

Su Yun held his breath.

The Xiāo watched her for a long time. Then, slowly, it lowered its head and took another drink.

"You may share my pool," it said. "For now."

The cub dipped her head and drank.

Su Yun's legs gave out. He sat down hard on the grass, his heart pounding, his hands shaking. He had just watched his half-starved cub stare down a fourth-rank beast and win.

Not with strength. With something else.

He didn't know what. But he knew it mattered.

---

Three hours later, Su Yun caught his first beast.

It wasn't the Xiāo—that one left after drinking, disappearing into the forest like smoke. But while the cub slept by the pool, a lesser beast wandered into the clearing: a Hǔ, a small tiger-like creature with stripes that shifted like shadows.

Su Yun didn't think. He moved.

The rope flew from his hand, looping around the Hǔ's hind leg. It snarled, twisting, but Su Yun held fast, pulling, using the tree behind him for leverage. The cub woke, saw what was happening, and padded over to stand beside him.

The Hǔ saw the cub. It stopped struggling.

Su Yun felt something click in his chest—a connection, like a lock turning. The beast's resistance melted, and he knew, with a certainty that surprised him, that it had accepted him as its tamer.

He had done it.

He had caught his first beast.

Su Yun knelt, breathing hard, and looked at the Hǔ. It looked back with amber eyes, its stripes rippling like liquid shadow.

"Hello," he said. "I'm Su Yun."

The Hǔ blinked. Then it padded over and rubbed against his leg.

Su Yun laughed. Laughed until his ribs ached, until tears streamed down his face. The cub watched him with what might have been amusement, her tail flicking.

For the first time in three days, he felt like maybe—just maybe—he could do this.

---

The celebration didn't last.

A roar split the forest—deep, primal, shaking the very ground. The cub's ears flattened. The Hǔ fled, disappearing into the undergrowth.

Su Yun turned.

The Xiāo stumbled into the clearing, its beautiful white coat streaked with blood. One of its legs was broken, bent at an impossible angle. Its horn was cracked, splintered.

It looked at Su Yun with those intelligent eyes, and for the first time, he saw something he hadn't expected.

Fear.

"Run," it said, its voice a whisper. "The Celestial Harmony Sect. They've found the Crimson Valley's heart."

"What—"

"They're coming for the Beasts of the Nine Realms." The Xiāo coughed, blood bubbling from its mouth. "And they're killing everything in their path."

It collapsed.

And in the distance, Su Yun heard it—the sound of drums. The sound of a sect marching to war.

The cub pressed against his leg, growling.

Su Yun looked at the fallen Xiāo. Looked at the cub. Looked at the forest that was suddenly, terrifyingly, no longer empty.

He had come here to catch beasts.

But the beasts were the least dangerous things in these woods.

title: The Beast That Shouldn't Be
content:
The tournament grounds stretched across three mountain valleys, each one carved by the claws of ancient beasts. Su Yun stood at the registration tent, the morning sun burning off the mist that clung to the grass. His rope was coiled at his hip. His Qiongqi cub pressed against his leg, growling at nothing.

"You sure about this, boy?" The registrar was a woman with iron-gray hair and eyes that had seen a thousand tamers come and go. She held a jade tablet, her thumb tracing characters that glowed with inner fire. "Entry fee is fifty spirit stones. Non-refundable. Even if you die in the first round."

Fifty. Su Yun's stomach dropped. He had twelve.

"Where do I sign up for the free-entry bracket?"

The registrar's eyes narrowed. "There is no free-entry bracket."

"Then where do the poor sign up?"

"Same place as the dead. The ground." She gestured to the valley behind her, where other tamers were already setting up camps. Their beasts were magnificent — scale and fur and feather, creatures that crackled with lightning or steamed with inner heat. A young man in blue silk walked past with a Bai Ze on a golden chain, the lion-bodied beast's single horn gleaming like polished ivory. It turned to look at Su Yun, and its ancient eyes held nothing but contempt.

The Qiongqi cub bared its teeth. A sound came from its throat — not a growl, but something deeper. Something that made the Bai Ze's ears flatten and its master yank the chain.

"Quiet, little one," Su Yun whispered, but the cub wouldn't stop.

The registrar was watching them now, her expression unreadable. "That beast. Where did you find it?"

"In the mountains."

"Liar."

Su Yun met her eyes. "It's the truth."

"The Qiongqi is a creature of chaos. It doesn't exist in mountains. It exists in the spaces between realms, in the cracks where order breaks down." She leaned forward. "You didn't find it. It chose you. And that's worse."

"Worse than what?"

"Worse than dying in the first round." She pressed her thumb to the jade tablet. "Fine. Free-entry bracket. But you'll start in the Pit, with the other desperate souls. If you survive the first night, come find me. I'll remember your face."

She didn't say what would happen if he didn't survive.

---

The Pit was a crater at the base of the central valley, ringed by cliffs that caught the sun and turned the air to wet heat. Fifty tamers huddled in the shade, their beasts thin and their eyes hollow. They were the ones who couldn't afford spirit stones. The ones who'd staked everything on a single throw of the dice.

A man with a scarred face and a three-legged wolf came up to Su Yun. "New meat?"

"New meat."

"You know the rules?"

"Survive."

The man laughed, but there was no humor in it. "That's not the rule. The rule is: don't be the weakest. Every night, they send down one beast from the upper brackets. A real beast. A blood beast. We fight it as a group. The one who contributes least gets thrown out. And thrown out means fed to the beast."

Su Yun looked around the Pit. Fifty tamers. By the end of the first week, half of them would be gone. By the end of the tournament, maybe three would remain.

"How do they judge contribution?"

"Damage. Healing. Control. The tablet knows." The man pointed to a giant jade slab at the edge of the crater, its surface covered in names and numbers. "Your name's already on it. Su Yun. Zero contribution. Zero spirit stones. Zero everything."

The Qiongqi cub sniffed the three-legged wolf. The wolf growled. The cub growled back. The wolf's growl faltered.

"Interesting beast," the scarred man said. "What is it?"

"A cat."

"That's not a cat."

"It's a cat."

The man stared at him for a long moment, then shrugged and walked away. Su Yun found a spot against the cliff wall, where the shade was deepest and the ground was dry. He sat down. The cub curled in his lap, its body trembling with a heat that had nothing to do with the sun.

"Hungry?" he asked.

The cub's eyes opened. They were the color of rust and blood and dying fires.

"I know. Me too."

---

The sun fell behind the cliffs. The heat faded, replaced by a cold that crept up from the earth itself. The other tamers built fires. Su Yun had no wood, no flint, no nothing. He sat in the dark, the cub pressed against his chest, and listened to the sounds of the tournament.

From the upper valleys came roars that shook the ground. Explosions of light painted the night sky — fire and lightning and something that looked like stars falling. The real tamers were fighting real battles. The real beasts were earning real glory.

Down in the Pit, the desperate ones huddled together and prayed.

The first beast came at midnight.

A bell rang. The jade tablet flared to life, casting pale light across the crater. A name appeared: HUO SHA, RED SAND. Contribution required: 100 points.

And then the wall of the Pit collapsed.

Dust and stone exploded inward as something massive pushed through. It was a Xiezhi — a beast of judgment, its body covered in black scales, its single horn burning with blue fire. It was thirty feet long, its claws leaving furrows in the stone as it dragged itself into the crater.

The tamers screamed. Some ran. Some threw themselves at the beast, their attacks bouncing off its scales like rain on rock.

The scarred man's wolf lunged. The Xiezhi's tail caught it mid-air, sent it crashing into the cliff wall. The man cried out, ran to his beast, and the Xiezhi ignored him. It wasn't interested in the weak.

It was interested in the cub.

The Xiezhi's head turned. Its blue-flame horn flared, and its eyes locked onto Su Yun. Onto the Qiongqi in his arms.

"Little one," Su Yun whispered. "Don't move."

The cub didn't listen.

It climbed out of his lap, its tiny claws digging into the dirt. It walked toward the Xiezhi — walked, not ran, not crept — walked like it owned the ground beneath its feet. The Xiezhi lowered its head, its horn aimed at the cub. Blue fire dripped from the tip, sizzling against the stone.

The cub stopped.

It opened its mouth.

And it screamed.

The sound was not the cry of a cub. It was the cry of something older, something that had existed before the mountains, before the realms, before the beasts that ruled them. It was the sound of chaos made flesh, of order breaking down, of the spaces between realms tearing open.

The Xiezhi's horn flickered. Its scales rippled. It took a step back.

The cub screamed again, and this time the ground cracked. Cracks spread from its feet, racing across the crater floor, spider-webbing up the cliff walls. The other tamers fell. The scarred man grabbed his wolf and pulled it away. The jade tablet flickered, numbers scrambling, names blurring.

And then a hand grabbed Su Yun's shoulder.

"Come with me. Now."

The registrar. Her iron-gray hair was wild, her eyes wide. She pulled him away from the cub, away from the chaos, toward a tunnel that had opened in the cliff wall.

"The cub—"

"The cub will follow. It always does." She dragged him into the darkness. The tunnel sloped upward, then opened into a cave lit by a single lantern. The registrar let go of him, breathing hard.

"That beast," she said. "You have no idea what you've bonded with."

"I know it's a Qiongqi."

"You know nothing." She stepped closer. "The Qiongqi is not a beast. It's a symptom. A crack in the fabric of the Nine Realms. Every hundred thousand years, one bonds with a tamer. And every hundred thousand years, that tamer dies. Because the Qiongqi doesn't tame. It consumes."

The cub padded into the cave. Its eyes were normal now — black, wide, innocent. It rubbed against Su Yun's leg, purring.

"You're wrong," Su Yun said. "It's just a cub."

"It's a cub now. But it's growing. And when it grows, it will need to feed. On spirit energy. On beasts. On tamers. On everything." The registrar's voice dropped. "You entered this tournament to save your village. But you're not a savior, boy. You're the disaster that's coming."

Behind her, the cub's shadow stretched across the cave wall.

And in that shadow, something else moved.

Something that had no shape. No form. No name.

It watched Su Yun with eyes that didn't exist, and it smiled.

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Shadows That Watched

Su Yun stared at the shadow on the cave wall. It stretched and twisted, separate from the cub's tiny form, a dark stain that moved with intentions the cub did not have.

"What is that?" he whispered.

The registrar didn't answer. She was backing away slowly, her hand reaching for something at her belt — a small bronze bell, its surface covered in characters Su Yun couldn't read.

The shadow grew. It detached from the cub's feet, rising like smoke, forming a shape that was almost human but not quite. Too many limbs. Too many angles. It pressed against the cave wall, and the wall groaned.

"Don't move," the registrar said. "Don't breathe. Don't acknowledge it."

The cub looked up at Su Yun and mewed.

The shadow paused.

Then it flowed back into the cub, sinking into its fur, disappearing like water into sand. The cub blinked. It yawned, showing tiny needle teeth, and curled up at Su Yun's feet.

The cave was still.

Su Yun's heart hammered against his ribs. "What. Was. That."

The registrar lowered her hand from the bell. Her face was pale, her breathing shallow. "You've seen it now. The shadow that follows the Qiongqi. The part of it that doesn't belong to this realm."

"I thought you said the Qiongqi was the crack between realms."

"It is." She walked to the lantern and turned up the flame. Light pushed against the darkness, but the corners of the cave stayed stubbornly black. "And cracks have two sides. The Qiongqi is the side that faces our world. The shadow is the side that faces... the other."

"Other what?"

She looked at him. For a moment, her iron-gray hair seemed to shimmer, like it was made of something other than hair. Then it was normal again.

"Other everything."

Su Yun wanted to ask more, but a low rumble shook the cave. Dust fell from the ceiling. Somewhere above them, voices shouted, and stones clattered down the tunnel.

"The tournament," the registrar said. "They're still fighting. They haven't noticed we're gone."

"They will."

"Not for a while. The Xiezhi's rampage will keep them busy." She picked up the lantern. "We need to move. The other tamers will report what they saw. By nightfall, every sect in the Nine Realms will know a Qiongqi cub appeared in the tournament. And they'll come looking."

"For the cub?"

"For you." She grabbed his arm. "A Qiongqi bonded to a tamer is a weapon. The most powerful weapon in the Nine Realms. And weapons don't get to choose their owners."

She led him through a narrow passage behind the cave. The walls pressed close, rough stone scraping his shoulders. The cub followed, its claws clicking on the rock.

They emerged into a forest. Moonlight filtered through dense canopy, casting silver patterns on the ground. The trees were ancient, their trunks wider than houses, their roots forming walls that blocked the path behind them.

"Where are we?" Su Yun asked.

"The Winding Woods. Three hundred miles from the tournament grounds."

"How—"

"I know shortcuts." She didn't explain further. "We have until dawn before the sects mobilize. I can get you to a safe house, but after that, you're on your own."

"Why are you helping me?"

She stopped. Turned. The lantern light carved harsh shadows across her face. "Because I've seen this before. A hundred thousand years ago, I saw another boy with a Qiongqi. I couldn't save him."

"A hundred thousand—"

"You ask too many questions." She started walking again. "Keep up."

They moved through the forest in silence. The cub stayed close to Su Yun's heels, its ears twitching at every sound. Twice, it stopped and stared into the darkness, and both times, Su Yun saw the shadow flicker at the edges of his vision.

The safe house was a shack hidden in the hollow of a dead tree. Its walls were made of bones — beast bones, from creatures Su Yun didn't recognize — and its roof was thatched with dried scales. Inside, a single room held a bed of leaves, a cold firepit, and a shelf of jars filled with things that moved.

The registrar lit a fire with a snap of her fingers. The flames were blue, like the Xiezhi's horn. "Rest. We leave at dawn."

"You didn't tell me your name."

She sat by the fire, her back to him. "You don't need to know it."

"I need to know something. You said the Qiongqi consumes. That it will need to feed on spirit energy. On beasts. On tamers." He looked down at the cub, sleeping peacefully in his lap. "What happens when it needs to feed on me?"

The registrar was silent for a long time. The fire popped and crackled.

"When a Qiongqi consumes its tamer," she said finally, "it doesn't kill them. Not exactly. It takes everything that makes them who they are — their memories, their emotions, their soul — and replaces it with itself. The tamer becomes a shell. A vessel. The Qiongqi pilots them through the world, wearing their skin like a cloak."

Su Yun's blood went cold.

"How long do I have?"

"Depend on how fast it grows. A month. A year. Maybe less."

"Can I stop it?"

She turned to face him. The firelight made her eyes look hollow. "No one has ever stopped it. Not in a hundred thousand years. Not in a million. The Qiongqi always wins."

"Then why bother saving me?"

"Because you're the first tamer who asked about the shadow." She held his gaze. "The first who saw it and didn't run. The first who looked at that thing and saw something worth protecting."

"I saw a cub."

"You saw a cub." She nodded slowly. "Maybe that makes you different. Or maybe you're just too stupid to know better."

The cub stirred in his lap. It opened its eyes — black, deep, ancient — and looked at Su Yun. For a moment, he felt something pass between them. Not words. Not feelings. Something older. Something that existed before language.

Then the cub yawned and went back to sleep.

Su Yun didn't sleep. He sat by the fire, watching the cub's shadow dance on the wall, waiting for it to move on its own again. It didn't. But he felt it watching him. Waiting.

Dawn came gray and cold. The registrar was already standing, packing the jars into a leather bag. "The sects will have scouts in the woods by now. We need to move."

They left the shack. The forest was quiet — too quiet. No birds. No insects. Just the sound of their footsteps and the cub's breathing.

They walked for an hour. The trees grew thicker, the roots higher, until they were climbing over walls of wood and earth. The cub struggled but kept up, its tiny claws leaving scratches on the bark.

Then the registrar stopped.

"Trouble," she whispered.

Ahead, the forest opened into a clearing. In the center stood a man in white robes. His hair was silver, his eyes were gold, and he carried no weapon. He didn't need one.

Behind him, a beast waited. It was the size of a horse, with the body of a lion, the wings of an eagle, and the tail of a serpent. Its fur shimmered with every color of the rainbow.

A Zouwu.

One of the rarest beasts in the Nine Realms. A creature that could travel a thousand miles in a single stride.

"Registrar," the man said. "I was wondering when you'd surface."

The registrar's hand went to her bronze bell. "Zhao Tian. I should have known they'd send you."

"The sect needs the Qiongqi, old friend. You know this."

"I know nothing of the sort."

Zhao Tian smiled. It was not a kind smile. "The boy doesn't have to die. Give us the beast, and he can walk free."

"Walk free where? To your labs? Your cages? The boy is bonded to the Qiongqi. Taking it from him will kill him."

"Necessary sacrifices."

The cub growled. It was a small sound, almost pathetic, but the Zouwu flinched. Its rainbow scales rippled, and it took a step back.

Zhao Tian's smile vanished. "What did you do to it?"

"Nothing." The registrar pulled out her bell. "But it knows what you are."

"I am the one who will save the Nine Realms from the chaos you've unleashed." He raised his hand. The Zouwu crouched, its muscles coiling.

"Su Yun," the registrar said. "When I say run, you run. Don't look back. Don't stop. Run until you can't run anymore, and then run some more."

"Run where?"

"Anywhere. Everywhere. Just run."

She rang the bell.

The sound was not a sound. It was a vibration that went through Su Yun's bones, through his teeth, through his soul. The air rippled. The trees groaned. The Zouwu screamed and stumbled.

And the shadow moved.

It rose from the cub's feet like a tidal wave. Black. Formless. Hungry. It spread across the clearing, blotting out the light, swallowing the color from everything it touched.

Zhao Tian's eyes went wide. "What—"

The shadow hit him.

He didn't scream. He didn't move. He simply stopped being. One moment he was there, the next he was gone — erased, like he had never existed.

The Zouwu bolted. The shadow flowed after it, faster than anything should be, and the beast vanished into the black.

Then the shadow turned.

Toward Su Yun.

He felt it reach for him. Felt its cold touch on his skin, his bones, his soul. It wanted him. Not to consume. Not to destroy.

To wear.

The cub screamed. The shadow froze.

Then it withdrew, sinking back into the cub's feet, shrinking until it was just a shadow again. Normal. Harmless.

The cub collapsed.

Su Yun caught it. Its body was cold, its breathing shallow. Its eyes were closed, and the spirit bond between them was faint — barely a whisper.

"What happened?" he asked.

The registrar's face was ashen. "It protected you. The shadow wanted you, and the cub pushed it back."

"It's dying."

"No." She knelt beside him, touching the cub's chest. "It's fighting. The Qiongqi and the shadow are two halves of the same whole. The cub is young. It doesn't have full control yet."

"Can I help it?"

She looked at him. For the first time, he saw something other than fear in her eyes. Something like hope.

"Maybe," she said. "But it will cost you everything."

"I don't have anything left to lose."

She reached into her bag and pulled out a jar. Inside, something glowed — a small, white light, pulsing like a heartbeat.

"This is a spirit core," she said. "From a creature that died before the realms were formed. If you feed it to the cub, it will grow stronger. It will learn to control the shadow."

"What's the cost?"

"You will never be able to bond with another beast. The Qiongqi will be your only companion. Your only weapon. Your only friend. For the rest of your life."

Su Yun looked at the cub. At the tiny creature that had screamed at a Xiezhi, that had pushed back a shadow from beyond realms, that had chosen him when it could have chosen anyone.

"Give it to me."

The registrar handed

title: The Shadow That Watches
content:
The lantern flickered.

Su Yun stared at the registrar, her words still echoing in his skull. *You're the disaster that's coming.* The cub rubbed against his leg, purring like an ordinary kitten, and he wanted to believe that was all it was. A cub. A mistake. A beast that shouldn't exist.

But the shadow on the wall behind her—that shapeless, nameless thing—it was still watching him.

"What are you talking about?" His voice came out rougher than he intended. "You said you'd help me. You said the tournament could save my village."

The registrar—he still didn't know her name—ran a hand through her iron-gray hair. She looked older now, the lines on her face deeper, the weight of something ancient pressing down on her shoulders.

"I said you could *enter* the tournament. I never said it would save anything." She walked to the lantern and turned the wick higher. The light pushed against the darkness, but the shadow on the wall didn't retreat. It *thickened*. "The Qiongqi bonds with a tamer once every hundred thousand years. Do you know what happened to the last one?"

Su Yun shook his head.

"His name was Yan Wu. The strongest beast tamer in the history of the Nine Realms. He captured a Vermilion Bird, a White Tiger, a Black Tortoise—three of the four sacred beasts. He was unbeatable." She paused. "Then the Qiongqi chose him."

"Chose him? I thought it bonded by accident."

"Nothing about the Qiongqi is accidental." The registrar's eyes met his. "Yan Wu didn't know what he had. He thought it was a gift. He trained it, fed it, watched it grow. For three years, he dominated every tournament, every battle, every realm. Then the Qiongqi grew hungry."

The cub stopped purring. It sat down, its black eyes fixed on the registrar.

"The Qiongqi consumed Yan Wu's other beasts first. The White Tiger lasted a day. The Vermilion Bird lasted three. The Black Tortoise held out for a week." Her voice dropped to a whisper. "Then the Qiongqi consumed Yan Wu himself. Not his body. His spirit. His cultivation. Everything he was. It left him a hollow shell, alive but empty, and then it moved on to find its next tamer."

"That's not going to happen." Su Yun picked up the cub. It felt warm in his arms, its heartbeat steady against his chest. "This one's different."

"They're all different. Until they're not."

The shadow on the wall shifted. It stretched, reaching toward Su Yun like a hand made of darkness. The cub's ears flattened. It growled—a low, guttural sound that didn't belong in such a small body.

The registrar's eyes widened. "It's already manifesting."

"Manifesting what?"

"The shadow. The *other thing* that lives inside the Qiongqi." She backed away from the wall. "The ancient texts call it the Gui. The ghost of the Qiongqi's past victims. Every beast the Qiongqi has ever consumed—their spirits are trapped inside it, waiting to be released."

The shadow's hand reached the cub. It touched its fur, and the cub screamed—the same scream from the crater, the sound of chaos made flesh. The cave shook. Dust fell from the ceiling. The lantern went out.

Darkness.

Su Yun held the cub tighter. He could feel its heart racing, its claws digging into his arms. Something moved in the darkness. Not the cub. Something bigger. Something that breathed.

*"Su Yun."*

The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. It was old. Ancient. The voice of a thousand beasts speaking as one.

*"You carry the Devourer."*

"Who are you?" Su Yun's voice cracked.

*"We are the consumed. The forgotten. The ones who became part of the Qiongqi."* The voice grew louder, pressing against his ears. *"We have been waiting for a new vessel. A new tamer. A new feast."*

The registrar's voice cut through the darkness: "Don't listen to it. It's trying to break you."

*"Break him? No, registrar. We are trying to save him."* The voice laughed—a horrible sound, like bones grinding together. *"The Qiongqi will consume him. It always does. But if he bonds with us—if he accepts the Gui—he can control it. He can become the master, not the meal."*

Su Yun's mind raced. The cub was still in his arms, still warm, still alive. But the shadow—the Gui—it was offering him power. The kind of power that could save his village. The kind of power that could destroy the corrupt sect that threatened everything he loved.

"How?" he asked.

"Don't!" the registrar shouted.

*"It's simple."* The shadow formed a face in the darkness—a woman's face, beautiful and terrible, her eyes burning with blue fire. *"Let us in. Open your spirit to the consumed. We will give you strength beyond anything the Nine Realms has ever seen. We will give you the power to crush your enemies, to save your village, to become a legend."*

"And what do you get?"

The face smiled. *"We get to live again. Through you. Through the Qiongqi. We get to taste the world once more."*

The cub squirmed in Su Yun's arms. It looked up at him, its black eyes wide and innocent, and for a moment, he saw something else in them. Fear. The cub was afraid. Not of the shadow. Not of the Gui. Of *him*.

It was afraid he would say yes.

The registrar grabbed his arm. "Boy, listen to me. The Gui is a parasite. It will use you, drain you, and discard you. The only way to control the Qiongqi is to bond with it fully—to become its true tamer. Not to invite the ghosts of its past into your soul."

*"She lies."* The shadow's face twisted, becoming a man's face, then a beast's, then a child's. *"She wants the Qiongqi for herself. She's a registrar. Her job is to catalog beasts for the tournament. Do you think she cares about your village? Your family? She cares about the tournament."*

"Is that true?" Su Yun turned to the registrar.

Her jaw tightened. "It's not—"

*"She has already sent a message to the tournament masters. Telling them about the Qiongqi. Telling them about you. They're coming, Su Yun. They're going to take your beast. They're going to take you. And when they're done, your village will burn."*

The registrar's face went pale. "That's not—I didn't—"

But Su Yun saw the truth in her eyes. She had sent a message. She had betrayed him.

The cub growled.

"Little one," Su Yun whispered. "What do I do?"

The cub didn't answer. It couldn't. But it pressed its head against his chest, and he felt its heart beating in time with his. They were bonded. They were connected. And the cub was telling him something without words.

*Trust me.*

The shadow reached for him again. *"Choose, tamer. Accept us, and become a god. Refuse us, and become a corpse."*

Su Yun closed his eyes.

He thought of his village. The old man who taught him to read. The children who played in the streets. The woman who fed him when his parents died. They were all counting on him. They were all waiting for him to come back with power, with money, with hope.

But at what cost?

He opened his eyes. "No."

The shadow froze. *"What?"*

"I said no." Su Yun held the cub tighter. "I don't need your power. I don't need your ghosts. I have my beast, and that's enough."

*"You fool. You absolute fool."* The shadow's face contorted with rage. *"You have no idea what you've rejected. You have no idea what's coming for you. The tournament masters will—"*

"Let them come."

The darkness shattered.

Light flooded the cave—not from the lantern, but from the cub. Its fur glowed with a faint golden light, pushing back the shadow, burning it away. The Gui screamed, a thousand voices crying out in pain, and then it was gone.

The registrar stared at the cub, her mouth open. "How—how did it do that?"

"I don't know," Su Yun said. "But I think it's been holding back. Protecting me. Protecting itself."

The cub's glow faded. It looked up at him, exhausted, and collapsed in his arms.

"We need to move," the registrar said. "I did send a message. I'm sorry. I thought—I thought they could help. But I was wrong. They're going to come for you, and they're not going to stop until they have the Qiongqi."

"Then I'll stop them."

"You can't. They have cultivators at the Nascent Soul level. They have beasts that can level mountains. You have a cub that just used up all its energy to fight a shadow."

Su Yun looked down at the sleeping cub. The registrar was right. He was weak. He was nothing.

But the cub had chosen him. And he wasn't going to let it down.

"Where do we go?"

The registrar walked to the back of the cave and pressed her hand against the wall. A hidden door slid open, revealing a tunnel that spiraled downward.

"The Underrealm," she said. "The tournament masters won't follow you there. It's too dangerous. Too unstable. But if you want to survive, if you want to grow strong enough to protect your village and your beast, it's your only option."

Su Yun stepped toward the tunnel. The air that rose from it was cold, damp, and smelled of something ancient.

"What's in the Underrealm?"

"Beasts that make the Xiezhi look like a house cat. Spirits that have been trapped for millennia. And the ruins of a civilization that tried to tame the Qiongqi before you."

She handed him a small lantern. "The last tamer who went down there—Yan Wu—he came back with the power to challenge the Nine Realms. He also came back broken."

Su Yun took the lantern. "I'm already broken. Might as well see what else can break me."

He stepped into the tunnel.

Behind him, the cub stirred. It opened its eyes, and for just a moment, they weren't black. They were golden. And they were looking at something Su Yun couldn't see.

Something that was following them into the darkness.

The door slid shut.

And the last thing Su Yun heard before the tunnel swallowed them was the registrar's voice:

"May the beasts forgive you."

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER TITLE: The Shadow That Watches

The lantern flickered.

Su Yun stared at the cub's shadow on the cave wall. The thing moving inside it had no shape, no edges, no definition—just a writhing darkness that seemed to breathe. The cub kept purring, rubbing against his leg, oblivious.

"What is that?" Su Yun's voice came out hoarse.

The registrar didn't answer. She was staring at the shadow too, her hand pressed against her mouth. Her iron-gray hair had come loose from its bun, strands sticking to her sweat-slicked forehead.

"I've only seen it twice before," she whispered. "Once in the archives. Once in a memory crystal from the Third Realm War."

"Seen what?"

"The Qiongqi's shadow self." She lowered her hand. Her fingers trembled. "Every beast in the Nine Realms has a spirit core. A center of consciousness. But the Qiongqi doesn't have one. It has a void. And that void leaks."

The thing in the shadow stopped moving.

It turned—turned, not moved, because it had no body to turn with—and looked at Su Yun. He felt its gaze like cold water down his spine. The cub's purring intensified, as if responding to something only it could hear.

"Leaks where?" Su Yun asked.

"Everywhere." The registrar stepped backward, putting the lantern between herself and the shadow. "Into other realms. Into dreams. Into the spaces between death and waking. The Qiongqi doesn't just consume beasts and tamers. It consumes reality. Slowly. One bite at a time."

The cub stopped purring.

It looked up at Su Yun with those innocent black eyes, and for a moment—just a moment—he saw something ancient looking back. Something that had watched stars die and mountains crumble. Something that had been waiting.

Then the cub yawned, showing tiny needle-teeth, and the ancient thing was gone.

"You're lying," Su Yun said. "Or you're wrong. It's just a cub. It eats meat and sleeps on my chest. It doesn't consume reality."

"Not yet." The registrar's voice hardened. "But the shadow self is already forming. That means your bond is deepening faster than any recorded case. The last tamer to bond with a Qiongqi had thirty years before the shadow appeared. You've had thirty days."

Su Yun looked down at the cub. It had curled up around his foot, its tiny chest rising and falling with sleep. It looked so small. So fragile. So harmless.

"Why did you save me?" he asked. "Back in the crater. You pulled me into this tunnel. You could have let the Xiezhi kill me."

The registrar was silent for a long moment.

Then she reached into her robe and pulled out a folded piece of paper. Yellowed, cracked, tied with red string. She held it out to Su Yun.

He took it. Unfolded it.

It was a drawing. Crude, childlike, done in charcoal. It showed a boy standing on a cliff, holding something in his arms. Below the cliff, a river of beasts—thousands of them, their forms barely sketched but their eyes clearly drawn, each one different, each one watching the boy. Above the boy, a sky full of stars that were not stars. They were holes. Holes in the fabric of the sky, through which something dark and vast was pushing.

At the bottom of the drawing, in shaky calligraphy: "The Beastmaster of the Nine Realms."

Su Yun's hands went cold.

"Where did you get this?"

"My daughter drew it. Three years ago." The registrar's voice cracked. "She was five. She couldn't read. She couldn't write. But she drew this, and she drew it exactly once. Then she burned it. When I asked her why, she said—" The registrar stopped. Swallowed. "She said the thing in the sky told her to."

The cub stirred in its sleep. Its shadow stretched across the cave wall, and for a moment, the shapeless thing inside it seemed to grow larger.

"She went mad after that," the registrar continued. "Not screaming mad. Not violent. Quiet mad. She would stare at walls for hours. She would whisper to empty rooms. And she would draw. Always the same drawing. Always you. Always the beasts. Always the holes in the sky."

"Where is she now?"

The registrar's face went pale. "She disappeared. Six months ago. The night before she disappeared, she drew one final picture." She pulled another folded paper from her robe. This one was newer, the charcoal still smudging.

Su Yun took it. Unfolded it.

The drawing showed a cave. A lantern. A woman with iron-gray hair. And a boy holding a cub. Above them, the cave ceiling had dissolved into darkness, and through that darkness, something was reaching down.

Something with hands.

Hundreds of hands.

And in the center of the hands, a face. A face made of shadows and pain and hunger. A face that looked exactly like the cub.

"This is now," Su Yun whispered.

"This is now." The registrar's voice was barely audible. "She drew this the night she vanished. I found it on her bed the next morning. She drew the moment of your arrival in this cave before you ever arrived."

The cub's eyes snapped open.

It wasn't sleeping anymore. Its black eyes had turned red. Deep red. The red of blood mixed with darkness. It stood up, its tiny claws digging into the dirt, its fur bristling.

The shadow on the wall convulsed.

The shapeless thing inside it began to take form. Arms first. Then legs. Then a torso. Then a head. It pulled itself out of the shadow like a man climbing out of a pit, except it had no skin, no features, no eyes—only a mouth where its face should be. A mouth full of teeth that went on forever, row after row, disappearing into a throat that led nowhere.

The cub growled.

The shadow thing opened its mouth.

And sound came out. Not a roar. Not a scream. A word. One word, spoken in a language that had never been human, that had never been beast, that had never belonged to any realm.

Su Yun understood it anyway.

*MINE.*

The cub launched itself at the shadow thing.

It was absurd. A tiny ball of fur and claws against a creature of darkness that towered twice Su Yun's height. The cub hit the shadow thing's chest and passed straight through, tumbling across the cave floor. It scrambled up, shook itself off, and charged again.

"Stop!" Su Yun grabbed for the cub, but his hands passed through it. Through it. His fingers tingled, like they'd touched static.

"The bond is solidifying," the registrar said, her voice urgent. "You're becoming one with the void. If this continues—"

The shadow thing reached for the cub.

Its hand closed around the cub's body.

The cub screamed.

Not the ancient scream from the crater. A real scream. A pain scream. A scream that tore through Su Yun's chest like a blade, because he felt it too. He felt the shadow thing's fingers closing around his own heart, squeezing, crushing—

"No!"

He didn't think. He moved. He grabbed the nearest thing—the lantern—and threw it at the shadow thing.

The lantern shattered against the shadow thing's chest.

Fire exploded.

Not normal fire. Spirit fire. The lantern had been filled with oil infused with cultivator's energy, and when it touched the shadow thing, it burned like sunlight touching frost. The shadow thing shrieked—a sound that shook the cave, that made dust rain from the ceiling, that sent cracks racing through the stone walls.

It released the cub.

The cub collapsed, whimpering, its fur singed, its side bleeding.

Su Yun scooped it up. It weighed almost nothing. It felt like it was dying.

The shadow thing was retreating, pulling itself back into the cub's shadow, but it was fighting. Its hands clawed at the cave floor, leaving gouges in the stone. Its mouth kept opening and closing, making sounds that were almost words but not quite.

The registrar grabbed Su Yun's arm. "We have to go. Now. There's a passage behind that wall—"

The cave ceiling cracked.

Stone fell. Huge chunks, sharp as blades. The registrar pushed Su Yun toward a crack in the back wall, and they squeezed through just as the ceiling collapsed behind them, sealing the cave forever.

They emerged into another tunnel. Narrower. Darker. The smell of damp earth and something metallic.

The registrar lit a new lantern—where had she gotten it?—and held it up.

The tunnel stretched ahead, sloping downward. Walls covered in markings. Old markings. Not Shan Hai script. Not any script Su Yun recognized.

"What are these?" he asked.

The registrar studied them. Her face went gray. "They're warnings. From the First Realm War. The war that ended the Age of Beasts."

"Warnings about what?"

She pointed to a symbol carved into the stone. A circle with a smaller circle inside it. And inside that, a spiral.

"The Qiongqi," she said. "This symbol means 'the devourer of boundaries.' In the First Realm War, a Qiongqi bonded with a tamer who tried to use it to destroy the barriers between realms. It worked. For three days, the realms bled into each other. Beasts from the Ninth Realm walked the earth of the First. Spirits from the Fourth drowned the cities of the Sixth. And at the center of it all, the Qiongqi's tamer stood, screaming, unable to stop what he had started."

"What happened?"

"The tamer was killed. By his own beast. The Qiongqi consumed him—body, soul, and spirit core—and then it fell asleep. For a hundred thousand years, it slept. And when it woke, it was a cub again. Small. Innocent. Waiting for a new tamer." The registrar looked at the cub in Su Yun's arms. "Waiting for you."

The cub stirred. Its eyes were black again. Normal. Innocent.

It looked up at Su Yun and licked his chin.

"I don't believe in fate," Su Yun said. "I believe in choices. I chose to bond with this cub. I choose to keep it. And I choose to win this tournament, save my village, and prove everyone wrong."

The registrar stared at him.

For a long moment, neither of them spoke.

Then the cub's shadow twitched.

The registrar's lantern went out.

And in the darkness, something whispered.

*"You cannot choose what has already chosen you."*

Su Yun's blood turned to ice.

The whisper came from everywhere and nowhere. From the walls. From the floor. From inside his own skull.

The cub's shadow was moving again. But this time, it wasn't forming a shadow thing. It was forming words. Words made of darkness, crawling across the stone floor like living ink.

*"The tournament."*

*"The village."*

*"The realms."*

*"All of it will burn."*

*"And you."*

*"You will hold the match."*

The cub whined. It pressed its face against Su Yun's chest, trembling.

Su Yun looked down at the words. At the shadow that should not exist. At the beast that should not be.

And he thought of his village. Of the corrupt sect. Of the tournament. Of the nine realms.

He thought of the drawing. The boy on the cliff. The holes in the sky.

He thought of the registrar's daughter. Gone. Vanished. Because she had seen this moment. This exact moment.

"Then I'll be the best damn match-holder the realms have ever seen," Su Yun said.

The

title: The Weight of a Hundred Thousand Years
content:
Su Yun did not sleep that night.
