The cub lay curled in his lap, its tiny ribcage rising and falling with each breath. Innocent. Helpless. A creature that had shattered a crater and made a Xiezhi retreat. The registrar sat across the cave, her back against the stone wall, her eyes never leaving them.

"Tell me everything," Su Yun said.

The registrar—her name was Old Wen, she had told him, though she refused to say more—picked at a loose thread on her sleeve. "The Qiongqi appears once every hundred thousand years. The records say it's born from the death of a realm. When a realm collapses, when its energy disperses, something coalesces in the void. That something is the Qiongqi."

"That's not in any text I've read."

"Of course it isn't." Old Wen's laugh was hollow. "The sects burned those texts. The ones who knew the truth either died or were silenced. I was supposed to be silenced."

She pulled down the collar of her robe. A scar ran from her collarbone to her throat—jagged, puckered, the mark of claws that had raked deep.

"The last tamer was my master."

Su Yun's hands stilled on the cub's fur. "What happened?"

"He bonded with the Qiongqi when it was a cub, just like you. He thought he could control it. Train it. Use its power to heal the rift between realms." Old Wen's voice dropped to a whisper. "The Qiongqi grew. And when it grew, it needed more than spirit energy. It needed life. It consumed my master first—his cultivation, his memories, his soul. Then it consumed the sect. Then the mountain. Then the valley."

"Consumed?"

"Not eaten. *Consumed.* Everything that was became part of the Qiongqi. The trees, the rivers, the people—they didn't die. They just... stopped being themselves. They became extensions of the beast. Walking, breathing puppets with no will of their own."

The cub stirred. Its eyes opened—black, wide, innocent—and it yawned, showing tiny fangs. It looked up at Su Yun and purred.

Old Wen's face went pale. "It's already bonded deeper than my master's did. After a month, it barely acknowledged him. Yours won't leave your side."

"Because it trusts me."

"Because it's marking you." She stood, pacing the cave. "The Qiongqi doesn't bond. It claims. Every day you spend with it, every moment you feed it, every time you protect it—you're not building a connection. You're surrendering pieces of yourself."

Su Yun looked down at the cub. It had closed its eyes again, its tiny claws kneading his leg, a soft rumble in its chest. He thought of the way it had walked toward the Xiezhi, the way it had screamed, the way the ground had cracked. He thought of the shadow that moved in its shadow, the shapeless thing with nameless eyes.

"You said it grows," Su Yun said. "How long?"

"Depends on what it consumes. Spirit beasts accelerate the process. Tamers even more so. If it feeds on a high-level beast, it could mature in months instead of years."

"And if it doesn't feed?"

"It will feed anyway. On you."

The cub's purring stopped. It opened its eyes again, and for a fraction of a second, Su Yun saw something in them that wasn't a cub. Something ancient. Something hungry.

Then it blinked, and it was just a cub again.

---

The tournament continued.

The morning bell rang across the valley, summoning the remaining tamers to the arena. Su Yun counted fewer than half of the original participants. The crater had been sealed, the Xiezhi driven back into the mountains, but the fear lingered. Tamers clustered in groups, their beasts restless, their eyes darting at shadows.

Old Wen had given him a choice.

"Leave the cub here. I'll keep it contained. You can fight the tournament alone, win the prize, save your village. The cub will weaken without you, and I'll find a way to destroy it before it matures."

"You said it can't be destroyed."

"I said no one has succeeded. That's different."

Su Yun had looked at the cub, sleeping in a nest of torn robes. It had looked so small. So fragile. He thought of the villagers—Old Chen, the children, the woman who had given him her last bowl of rice. He thought of the sect that had taken everything, the taxes that had stripped them bare, the threats that had driven them to the edge of starvation.

And he thought of the cub's scream, the ground cracking, the Xiezhi retreating.

"Destroying it won't bring back your master," Su Yun had said.

Old Wen's face had hardened. "Neither will keeping it."

But she hadn't stopped him when he picked up the cub and walked toward the arena.

---

The first match was announced: Su Yun versus a tamer from the Azure Peaks Sect.

The tamer was young, barely sixteen, with a golden crane that stood twice his height. Its feathers shimmered, its beak glinted like steel, and its eyes held the cold intelligence of a beast that had killed before.

The crowd murmured. The odds were posted on a spirit board: Su Yun at fifty-to-one, the Azure Peaks tamer at even.

The cub sat on Su Yun's shoulder, watching the crane with unblinking eyes.

"Your beast isn't even registered," the young tamer said. "You're fighting with a pet."

Su Yun said nothing.

The bell rang.

The crane moved faster than its size suggested. Its wings snapped open, sending a wave of wind that knocked Su Yun off balance. Its beak stabbed forward—aimed at his chest, not his throat. A warning. A show of mercy.

The cub didn't scream this time.

It jumped.

It was so small that the crane didn't register it as a threat. The cub landed on the crane's wing, its tiny claws digging into the feathers. The crane screeched, shaking its wing, but the cub held on. It crawled up the crane's neck, toward its head, toward its eyes.

The young tamer shouted commands. The crane bucked, twisted, slammed its body against the arena wall. The cub held on.

And then it bit.

Not hard. Not deep. Just a pinch, really—a cub's bite, barely breaking skin.

But the crane stopped.

Its eyes went blank. Its wings drooped. It stood motionless, like a statue, like a puppet with its strings cut.

The cub hopped off, landing lightly on the ground, and trotted back to Su Yun.

"What did you do?" the young tamer screamed. "What did you do to my crane?"

The crane didn't move. Didn't blink. Its chest rose and fell, but slowly, as if it were dreaming a dream from which it might never wake.

The crowd was silent.

The judge—a woman from the Heavenly Mandate Sect—stepped forward, her face unreadable. "Match to Su Yun. The opponent's beast is incapacitated."

"No!" the young tamer grabbed his crane's leg, shaking it. "Wake up! Wake up!"

But the crane didn't wake.

Su Yun looked at the cub. It was cleaning its paw, as if nothing had happened.

*It consumed,* he realized. *Not the whole beast. Just a piece of it. Just enough to make it stop.*

The cub looked up at him, purred, and rubbed against his ankle.

---

Old Wen found him after the match.

"The consuming has begun," she said. "That crane will never fight again. Its spirit is broken, its will shattered. It's a shell now. A living corpse."

"I didn't tell it to do that."

"It doesn't need your permission. It acts on instinct. And its instinct is to consume." She grabbed his arm. "Do you understand now? Every match, every fight, it will take more. It will leave a trail of broken beasts and broken tamers. And when the tournament ends, when there's nothing left to consume, it will turn to you."

Su Yun pulled his arm free. "Then I'll find another way. I'll control it."

"No one controls the Qiongqi."

"Your master didn't. That doesn't mean I can't."

Old Wen's eyes softened. "You sound like him. He said the same thing. Right before it consumed him."

She turned and walked away, her iron-gray hair catching the wind.

---

The third match was announced.

Su Yun versus the scarred man from the Black Iron Sect.

The man's wolf was gone, replaced by a serpent—a Jiaolong, its body thick as a tree trunk, its scales black as ink. It coiled in the arena, its head swaying, its forked tongue tasting the air.

The scarred man smiled. "I saw what your beast did to the crane. It won't work on mine."

The cub sat on Su Yun's shoulder. Its eyes were fixed on the Jiaolong.

The bell rang.

The Jiaolong struck fast—faster than the crane, faster than anything Su Yun had faced. Its body uncoiled like a whip, its jaws gaping wide, aiming for the cub.

Su Yun grabbed the cub and rolled.

The Jiaolong's teeth closed on empty air. It twisted, its tail sweeping, catching Su Yun in the ribs. He flew backward, slammed into the arena wall, and the cub tumbled from his grasp.

"Little one!"

The cub landed on its feet. It faced the Jiaolong, its tiny body trembling, its fur bristling.

The scarred man laughed. "It's just a cub. A mouse. My Jiaolong will swallow it whole."

The cub opened its mouth.

But instead of screaming, it coughed.

Something small and dark fell from its mouth—a fragment of something, no bigger than a pebble. It clattered on the stone floor.

The Jiaolong paused. Its head tilted, curious.

The fragment pulsed.

And the shadow behind the cub grew.

It stretched across the arena, swallowing light, swallowing sound. The Jiaolong hissed, recoiling, but the shadow was faster. It wrapped around the serpent's body, climbed its scales, poured into its nostrils, its eyes, its open mouth.

The Jiaolong convulsed. Its body twisted, its scales flaking away, its flesh dissolving into motes of black light.

And then it was gone.

Not dead. Not consumed. *Gone.*

The scarred man stared at the empty space where his beast had been. His mouth opened. Closed. Opened again.

The shadow retreated, shrinking back into the cub's shadow, and the cub padded over to the fragment on the floor.

It picked up the fragment in its mouth. Swallowed it.

Then it hopped onto Su Yun's lap and purred.

The crowd was screaming.

The judges were shouting.

And Su Yun held the cub, his hands shaking, as he realized what Old Wen had meant.

The Qiongqi didn't just consume.

It unmade.

And it was only getting started.

title: The Shadow That Hungers
content:
The lantern flickered. The registrar's face was a mask of stone, but her hands trembled at her sides.

Su Yun stared at the cub. It sat at his feet, tail curling, completely oblivious to the weight of her words. *Every hundred thousand years, that tamer dies.*

"I don't believe you," he said.

"You don't have to." She reached into her robe and pulled out a scroll—yellowed, brittle, sealed with black wax. "I was there the last time."

"You're lying. You said every hundred thousand years."

"I said *every hundred thousand years.* I didn't say I was human back then."

The cub stopped purring. Its head lifted, ears rotating forward, eyes fixed on the scroll.

The registrar broke the seal. The scroll unrolled itself, revealing characters that burned red, then faded, then burned again. They were not written in any language Su Yun recognized. They moved. They *crawled* across the parchment like living things.

"This is the Accord of First Bonding," she said. "Signed by the first Qiongqi tamer and the wardens of the Nine Realms. It was meant to be destroyed. But I kept a copy."

"Why?"

"Because I knew you'd come." She held the scroll out to him. "Read the last line."

Su Yun took it. The characters squirmed under his fingers, hot, almost painful. He forced his eyes to focus, to trace the ancient script, and when he reached the bottom, his blood went cold.

*When the Qiongqi consumes its tamer, the bond breaks, and the realms mend. This is the only way.*

"That's not—" He dropped the scroll. It curled on the ground, the characters still writhing. "That's not what happens. The cub is bonded to me. It's not going to—"

"It already has." The registrar's voice was flat. "Look at your hand."

Su Yun looked down. His fingers were gray.

Not dirty. Not bruised. Gray, like ash, like stone, like something that had been drained of life. The color stopped at his wrist, a clean line separating living flesh from dead.

"When did this happen?" he whispered.

"Three days ago. When you first bonded. The consumption has already begun."

The cub looked up at him. Its eyes were black, innocent, hungry.

*Hungry.*

He had been feeding it spirit meat. He had been feeding it his own rations. He had been feeding it everything it wanted, and it had been eating him in return.

"No," he said. "There has to be another way. A way to stop it."

"There is." The registrar pointed at the scroll. "The Accord says the consumption can be reversed. If the Qiongqi bonds with a stronger tamer before the first moon cycle ends, the original bond breaks, and the consumption reverses."

"How long do I have?"

"Twenty days."

Twenty days. The tournament lasted thirty.

Su Yun's mind raced. If he could find a stronger tamer—someone powerful enough to take the bond—he could survive. He could go back to his village. He could—

And then the cub screamed.

Not the ancient, reality-tearing scream from the arena. This was a scream of fear. Of warning.

The lantern went out.

Darkness swallowed the cave, absolute and complete. Su Yun couldn't see his own hands, couldn't see the registrar, couldn't see the cub. But he could feel it—the thing from the shadow. The thing with no shape and no name.

It was here.

"Don't move," the registrar whispered. "Don't breathe. Don't—"

Something cold touched Su Yun's neck.

It wasn't a hand. It wasn't claws. It was *nothing*, a point of absolute cold that spread like frost across his skin, down his spine, into his chest. His heart slowed. His lungs stopped. His thoughts became thick, sluggish, like honey in winter.

*You are mine.*

The voice was not sound. It was thought, pressed directly into his skull, heavy and ancient and wrong.

*You are the vessel. You are the door. You opened it when you bonded.*

Su Yun tried to speak, but his mouth wouldn't move. His tongue was frozen. His throat was ice.

*The Qiongqi is the key. You are the lock. And I am what waits on the other side.*

The cold reached his heart. It wrapped around it, squeezed, and for a moment, Su Yun saw—not with his eyes, but with something deeper.

He saw a realm of endless gray. No sky. No ground. No horizon. Just gray, stretching in every direction, and in the center of it, a shape. A woman. Her hair was white, her eyes were black, and her smile was the hollow space between stars.

She raised her hand.

The gray realm shattered.

Su Yun gasped. He was on the ground, on his hands and knees, coughing, choking, his chest burning. The lantern was back on. The registrar stood over him, a knife in her hand, blood dripping from her palm.

"I cut the connection," she said. "It won't hold for long."

"What was that?" His voice was hoarse, broken.

"The thing the Qiongqi serves. The shadow between realms. The void that eats worlds." She knelt, grabbed his chin, forced him to look at her. "And now it knows you exist. It knows your name. It knows your village. It knows everyone you've ever loved."

"Then I'll break the bond. I'll find a stronger tamer."

"You don't have twenty days anymore." She released his chin. "You have ten. The void accelerates everything. Consumption. Growth. Death."

Ten days.

The cub pressed against his leg. Its fur was cold. Its eyes were black, and in their depths, Su Yun saw the faintest glimmer of gray.

He stood up. His legs were shaking. His gray fingers curled into a fist.

"Then I'll find a tamer in ten days."

"You'll have to win the tournament first." The registrar sheathed her knife. "The strongest tamers don't accept challenges from the weak. You have to prove yourself. You have to reach the summit of the Nine Peaks Arena. Only the top eight contenders can challenge for a bond transfer."

"The top eight." Su Yun looked at the cub. "How many are competing?"

"Three hundred and forty-seven."

Three hundred and forty-seven tamers. And he had to be one of the top eight in ten days.

"One more thing," the registrar said. "The void isn't patient. If you don't find a tamer by the end of the tenth day, it will take what it wants. And what it wants isn't just you. It's the Qiongqi. It's the bond. It's the door you opened."

The cub growled. Not at Su Yun. At the wall.

At the shadow on the wall.

The shadow of the cub was wrong. It was too large, too long, too *deep*. And in the shadow, the woman's smile flickered.

Su Yun turned away. He grabbed his rope, his bag, his half-starved cub.

"Where are you going?" the registrar asked.

"To find the strongest tamer in the tournament."

"You don't even know their names."

He stopped at the entrance of the cave. The moonlight spilled in, silver and cold, and the cub's shadow stretched behind him—too far, too dark, too hungry.

"I'll learn."

---

The tournament grounds were a fortress of stone and spirit energy. Walls of black obsidian rose a hundred feet high, etched with formation arrays that hummed with power. Guards patrolled the perimeter, their beasts walking beside them—wolves with scales, tigers with wings, serpents with three heads.

Su Yun walked through the main gate, the cub at his heels, and immediately felt the weight of a hundred eyes.

They were watching him.

The tamers from the arena. The ones who had seen the cub scream. The ones who had felt the ground crack, the air tear, the rules of reality bend.

They knew.

"The Qiongqi boy," someone whispered.

"He's still alive?"

"Look at his hand. He's already marked."

Su Yun kept walking. He found a notice board near the center of the grounds, covered in parchment. Tournament brackets. Betting odds. Bounty lists.

And at the top, in golden ink, a name:

**Elder Xuan—First Seat, Nine Peaks Arena—Undefeated in Thirty-Seven Tournaments**

The strongest tamer in the competition.

Su Yun reached up and tore the notice from the board.

"Heading somewhere?"

The voice was smooth, amused, and close.

Su Yun turned.

A young man stood behind him, no older than twenty, dressed in white silk embroidered with silver thread. A jade flute hung from his belt. His eyes were the color of honey, and his smile was the smile of a predator who had already won.

"I heard you were looking for a strong tamer." He tilted his head, examining Su Yun like a curious specimen. "I'm Elder Xuan."

Su Yun's hand tightened on the torn notice.

"Perfect," he said. "I challenge you."

The crowd went silent.

Elder Xuan laughed. It was not a cruel laugh, but it was not kind either. It was the laugh of someone who had heard the same joke a thousand times and was too polite to stop pretending it was funny.

"You don't challenge me," he said. "You survive until the final round, and then I decide if you're worth my time."

"I don't have time."

"Everyone has time. They just don't know how to use it."

Elder Xuan stepped closer. His presence was immense—not oppressive, but vast, like standing at the edge of a sea. His spirit energy coiled around him like a living thing, invisible but undeniable.

"You bonded with a Qiongqi," he said, his voice dropping to a whisper. "Do you know how many tamers have tried to bond with that beast and lived?"

"No."

"None. You're the first. That makes you interesting. But interesting doesn't mean strong." He tapped the flute against his palm. "Come find me at the summit. If you survive that long."

He turned and walked away, his white robes billowing.

Su Yun watched him go. The cub growled, low and dangerous.

"I know," Su Yun whispered. "But he's our only chance."

The cub looked up at him. Its eyes were black, innocent, hungry.

And in the reflection of its pupils, Su Yun saw the woman again.

She was standing behind him.

He spun around.

Nothing.

Just the crowd, the torches, the obsidian walls.

But the cold was back—the touch of nothing on his neck, the whisper of void in his ear.

*Ten days, little lock. Tick tock.*

Su Yun closed his eyes.

When he opened them, the cub was gone.

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Hole in the World

The lantern flame flickered, and the shadow on the wall vanished.

Su Yun blinked. His heart hammered against his ribs. The Qiongqi cub rubbed against his leg, its fur warm, its purring steady—the contented sound of a well-fed kitten, not the harbinger of annihilation the registrar claimed it to be.

"You're lying," he said, his voice steadier than he felt. "If the Qiongqi was that dangerous, the tournament wouldn't allow it. The rules say any beast can be bonded."

The registrar laughed. It was not a pleasant sound. It was the laugh of someone who had seen too much, who had buried too many secrets beneath too many lies.

"The tournament rules are written for tamers who bond with normal beasts. Wolves. Foxes. The occasional griffin. The Nine Realms Tournament has been held forty-three times. Forty-three times, a Qiongqi has appeared. Forty-three times, the registrars have been sworn to silence. Forty-three times, the bond has been broken by death."

"Broken how?"

"However it needed to be broken." She turned away, walking to a stone table at the back of the cave. Scrolls lay scattered across it, their edges yellowed, their seals cracked. She picked one up, unrolled it, and held it to the lantern light. "The first bonded tamer was a prince of the Azure Sky Realm. He was found three weeks after bonding, his body intact but his soul—gone. Empty. Like a husk that had been hollowed out by insects. The Qiongqi was found sleeping beside him, fat and content."

She let the scroll fall and picked up another.

"The second was a monk from the Eternal Ice Temple. He lasted six weeks. When they found him, he was still alive, but his memories had been eaten. He couldn't remember his own name, his own face, the faces of his disciples. He spent the rest of his life staring at a wall, drooling, while the Qiongqi grew stronger."

Another scroll.

"The third was a woman. A beast tamer of unmatched skill—she had tamed a Zhuque, a Vermilion Bird from the Southern Flame Domain. She bonded with the Qiongqi and lasted three months. When they found her, she was still fighting. But the Qiongqi had consumed her cultivation. Every drop of spirit energy she had ever gathered. She died an ordinary mortal, aged sixty years in a single night."

The registrar turned to face him. Her eyes were hard, but something flickered in their depths—something that looked almost like fear.

"Every thousand years, a Qiongqi appears. Every thousand years, a tamer bonds with it. And every thousand years, that tamer dies—and the Qiongqi is sealed away again, waiting for the next cycle."

"But it's just a cub," Su Yun said again, but the words felt weaker now. The cub, as if sensing his doubt, stopped purring. It sat at his feet and looked up at him with those wide, black eyes, and for a moment—just a moment—he thought he saw something ancient staring back.

"All cubs grow," the registrar said. "Your Qiongqi consumed a Xiezhi tonight. Not the beast itself—it's not strong enough for that yet—but its spirit energy. The fear it generated. The terror in the air. That's what it feeds on. Not meat. Not plants. Fear, boy. Chaos. The cracks between order and disorder."

She stepped closer, and her voice dropped to a whisper.

"You want to save your village. I understand that. I grew up in a village like yours—mud huts, starving children, corrupt sects squeezing every coin until the bones grind. But you cannot save them by becoming a monster. You cannot save them by bringing a disaster to their doorstep."

"Then what do I do?" The words came out raw, stripped of pretense. "I have nothing. No cultivation. No connections. No power. The Qiongqi is all I have."

The registrar stared at him for a long moment. Then she turned back to the table and picked up a fourth scroll—this one bound in red silk, sealed with black wax stamped with a symbol Su Yun didn't recognize.

"There is a way," she said. "A legend. A method that has never been attempted, because no tamer has survived long enough to try it."

She broke the seal. The scroll unfurled, and the characters on it seemed to writhe in the lantern light, shifting between languages, between meanings, between realities.

"The Qiongqi is a beast of chaos. It exists in the spaces between realms. But what if you could bind it not to your soul—but to your will? Not as a master binds a slave, but as a warrior binds a weapon? What if you could forge the chaos into something controlled?"

"That sounds impossible."

"It is." The registrar's mouth twisted. "The method requires three artifacts. The first is the Mirror of Wu, said to be hidden in the Sunken Temple of the Eastern Sea. The second is the Feather of Dapeng, a bird so large it blocks out the sun when it flies—the feather must be plucked while the bird is awake, which has never been done. And the third—"

She stopped.

"The third?"

"The third is the Eye of Hundun. The primordial chaos beast. The thing that existed before the realms were formed. The Qiongqi is a crack in reality. The Hundun is the hole that the crack leads to."

The cub tensed. Its ears flattened against its skull. A low growl rumbled in its chest—not the growl of a cub, but something deeper, something that made the cave walls vibrate.

"The Hundun is sealed at the center of the Nine Realms," the registrar continued, her voice barely audible over the growl. "In the Void Between. No one who has gone there has returned. But if you could retrieve its eye—if you could bind the Qiongqi with the essence of the very chaos it serves—then perhaps, perhaps, you could control it."

She rolled up the scroll and pressed it into his hands.

"Take it. Read it. Decide. But know this—once you start down this path, there is no turning back. The Qiongqi will grow, and it will hunger, and every moment you delay, it will consume more of you."

The cub stopped growling. It nuzzled against Su Yun's leg, and he felt a surge of warmth—of affection—that he knew, with sickening certainty, was not entirely his own.

"How long?" he asked. "How long until it consumes me?"

The registrar looked at him with something that might have been pity.

"Based on the records? You have eight weeks. Maybe ten, if you're lucky. The tournament lasts three months. By the final round, either you will have bound the Qiongqi, or the Qiongqi will have eaten everything you are."

She walked past him, toward the tunnel entrance.

"I've broken the rules by telling you this. The Covenant of Registrars requires silence. But I've seen too many tamers die, boy. Too many villages burn. Your village—I looked it up. Cloud's Rest. Population three hundred and twelve. Corrupt sect called the Iron Fist Brotherhood bleeding them dry. If you fail, they will be the first to fall."

She paused at the tunnel's mouth.

"Eight weeks. Use them wisely."

And she was gone.

The lantern flickered. The cub yawned, revealing tiny teeth that seemed sharper than they had been an hour ago. Su Yun stood alone in the cave, holding a scroll that felt heavier than any weapon, any treasure, any burden he had ever carried.

Eight weeks.

He thought of his mother's face. Of the elders who had raised him. Of the children who had laughed at his jokes and called him a fool for dreaming of becoming a beast tamer.

He thought of the Iron Fist Brotherhood. Of their demands. Their cruelty. Their casual destruction of everything he loved.

And he thought of the Qiongqi—the ancient chaos curled at his feet, purring like a kitten, waiting to be unleashed.

"I'm not going to be your meal," he said to the cub. "And I'm not going to let you destroy my home."

The cub looked up at him. Its eyes were black, but somewhere in their depths, something flickered—something that might have been amusement.

"Eight weeks," Su Yun said. "Let's start with the Mirror of Wu."

He rolled up the scroll, tucked it into his shirt, and walked out of the cave.

The tournament grounds were chaos. Tamers were milling about, their beasts restless, their faces pale. The scarred man was being carried away on a stretcher, his wolf limping beside him, its tail between its legs. The jade tablet still hung in the air, flickering, its numbers scrambled and meaningless.

And standing in the center of the chaos, waiting for him, was a figure Su Yun had never seen before.

She was tall—taller than any woman he had ever met—with skin as pale as moonlight and hair that shifted colors in the torchlight, flowing from silver to blue to black and back again. She wore robes of white silk embroidered with patterns that hurt to look at, spiraling geometries that seemed to pull the eye into their center and never let go.

Her eyes were not human.

They were deep, deep gold, with slit pupils like a cat's, and when she looked at Su Yun, he felt something cold crawl down his spine—something that had nothing to do with the night air.

"You're the one," she said. Her voice was soft, musical, and utterly wrong—like a bell that had been cracked and repaired, the sound just slightly off-key. "The one who bonded with the chaos cub."

The Qiongqi hissed. It pressed against Su Yun's leg, its tiny body trembling—not with fear, but with recognition.

"Who are you?" Su Yun asked.

The woman smiled. It was a beautiful smile. It did not reach her eyes.

"I am Li Wei," she said. "Former registrar of the Nine Realms Tournament. Current keeper of the Covenant's darkest secrets. And the woman who bonded with the last Qiongqi, a thousand years ago."

She lifted her hand, and the sleeve of her robe fell back, revealing a scar that ran from her wrist to her elbow—a scar that pulsed with black light, like a vein filled with ink.

"I survived," she said. "Barely. And I've been waiting ever since for the next fool to try what I attempted."

Her golden eyes locked onto his.

"I can help you, boy. But you need to understand one thing."

She stepped closer, and the cub growled, and the air between them crackled with something ancient and hungry.

"The Qiongqi doesn't consume tamers by accident. It consumes them because it's trying to fill a void that has existed since the beginning of time. A void that nothing can fill."

She leaned in, her breath cold against his ear.

"Except the one thing it was created to find."

Su Yun's blood turned to ice.

"What thing?"

Li Wei pulled back, and her smile widened—a predator's smile, hungry and patient and eternal.

"The Eye of Hundun," she said. "The chaos that birthed it. The hole in the world that the Qiongqi was born to seek."

She reached into her robe and pulled out a mirror. It was cracked, its surface webbed with fractures, but in its reflection, Su Yun saw something that made his heart stop.

He saw himself—but not as he was. He saw himself older, gaunt, his eyes hollow, his hands stained with blood. He saw the Qiongqi grown, massive, its wings blocking

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Debt of Ancient Blood

The tunnel swallowed them whole.

Su Yun ran behind the registrar, his legs burning, his lungs screaming. The Qiongqi cub kept pace at his heel, its claws clicking against stone, its breath coming in short, sharp bursts. Behind them, the tunnel collapsed—not slowly, not with warning, but in a single thunderous roar that shook the earth and sent dust billowing past them like a wave.

The registrar didn't stop. She grabbed his wrist and pulled him through a narrow crevice, then another, then a third, until the sound of falling rock faded to nothing.

They emerged into a chamber that shouldn't have existed.

It was circular, carved from obsidian that gleamed like wet ink. Pillars of bone rose from floor to ceiling, each one etched with characters so old they seemed to writhe under Su Yun's gaze. In the center of the chamber stood a stone altar, and on that altar lay a body.

No. Not a body.

A skeleton. Human. Clutching a bronze bell to its chest.

The registrar let go of his wrist. She walked to the altar, her footsteps echoing in the dead silence, and placed her hand on the skeleton's skull.

"His name was Wei Chen," she said. "The last tamer of a Qiongqi. He died here, in this chamber, three thousand years ago."

Su Yun's mouth went dry. "You said every hundred thousand years."

"I lied." She turned to face him. "The truth is worse. The Qiongqi bonds more frequently. Every thousand years, sometimes five hundred. But each time, the tamer dies. And each time, the records are buried. Burned. Erased. Because if people knew the truth, they would hunt every Qiongqi cub to extinction. And that cannot happen."

"Why not?"

"Because without the Qiongqi, the barrier between realms collapses." She gestured to the pillars. "This chamber is a seal. One of seven. The Qiongqi is not a beast—it's a keystone. It holds the Nine Realms together by existing. By binding to a tamer. By consuming."

The cub sat at Su Yun's feet. It looked up at him, its eyes black and depthless, and yawned.

"Consuming what?" Su Yun asked.

"Beasts. Spirit energy. Life force." The registrar's voice cracked. "And eventually, the tamer himself. Wei Chen lasted three years. He was the strongest tamer of his age, a Ninth Realm cultivator, a man who had faced dragons and walked away. The Qiongqi ate him from the inside out. First his cultivation. Then his memories. Then his soul."

She picked up the bronze bell from the skeleton's hands. It was tarnished, cracked, but when she shook it, no sound came out.

"By the end, Wei Chen didn't know his own name. He didn't know his wife. He didn't know the daughter who came to visit him every day. He sat in this chamber and rang this bell, over and over, because it was the only thing he remembered. And when he died, the Qiongqi vanished. Reborn as a cub somewhere else. Waiting for the next fool to bond with it."

Su Yun looked down at the cub. It blinked at him, innocent, trusting.

"It's not its fault," he said.

"No. It's not. But that doesn't change what it is." The registrar held out the bell. "Take it."

He took it. It was cold, heavier than it looked, and when his fingers touched the bronze, he felt something stir inside him. A presence. A pulse. Like a second heartbeat, deep in his chest.

"What is this?"

"A memory. Wei Chen's final message, locked in the bell. The Qiongqi will try to consume you. It cannot help itself. But if you understand what it truly is—if you understand the price it pays to exist—you might survive longer than he did." She paused. "You might even survive long enough to save your village."

The cub whined. It pawed at Su Yun's leg, its tiny claws catching on his trousers, and he knelt to pick it up. It nuzzled against his neck, warm and alive and so impossibly small.

"How long do I have?"

"I don't know. Months. Weeks. The bond accelerates as the cub grows. Every time it feeds, it takes a piece of you." She looked at the skeleton. "Wei Chen's Qiongqi fed once a day. By the end, he was a hollow shell. You have a choice, Su Yun. You can leave the tournament now, go back to your village, and live what time you have left. Or you can stay. You can fight. You can try to become strong enough that when the Qiongqi finally takes you, it takes something worth having."

He thought of the village. Of Old Chen, who had taught him to read. Of the children who followed him through the streets, begging for stories about beasts he'd never seen. Of his mother's grave, overgrown with weeds, because he couldn't afford to buy flowers.

"If I leave," he said, "the village dies anyway. The sect will take it. They'll turn the people into slaves, and the land into ash. At least this way, I have a chance."

The registrar's face softened. "You sound like him. Wei Chen. He said the same thing, before he entered the tournament. Before he bonded with the beast that killed him."

"Then maybe I'll end up like him." Su Yun held up the bell. "But I'll ring this thing until my last breath. And when I'm gone, someone else will pick it up. And they'll keep ringing it. Until the realms fall apart or we find another way."

The cub purred against his neck. Its warmth spread through him, soothing, seductive, and for a moment he understood why Wei Chen had stayed. Why he had let the Qiongqi consume him, piece by piece. Because the bond was not pain. It was pleasure. It was the feeling of being complete, of being more than yourself, of touching something vast and eternal.

"Tell me how to get back to the tournament," he said.

The registrar nodded. She walked to the far wall and pressed her hand against the obsidian. A door slid open, revealing a staircase that spiraled upward into torchlight.

"The arena is above. The second round has already begun. If you hurry, you might still be able to enter."

"Thank you." He stepped toward the stairs.

"Su Yun."

He turned.

The registrar's eyes were wet. "Don't trust the beasts. Don't trust the tamers. Don't trust anyone who offers you power." She looked at the skeleton. "And don't trust yourself. The Qiongqi will make you feel invincible. It will make you feel like you can do anything. But you can't. And the moment you believe you can, you're already dead."

He nodded. Then he climbed.

---

The arena was chaos.

Beasts fought in the center of a crater twice the size of the first one. A serpent of living flame coiled around a three-headed wolf. A Roc the size of a house tore at the sky with talons that left trails of light. The other tamers watched from the rim, their faces pale, their hands shaking.

A woman grabbed his arm as he emerged. "Where have you been? The round started an hour ago!"

"Got lost." He looked past her, scanning the arena. The scarred man was there, his wolf standing beside him, its fur singed and smoking. He caught Su Yun's eye and smiled—a smile that promised nothing good.

"Tamers!" The voice boomed across the arena. The announcer stood on a floating platform, his robes billowing in the heat. "The second round is almost over. But we have a surprise. A special challenge, for those who still have the courage to face it."

The ground shook. A crack split the arena floor, spreading outward, and from that crack rose a beast that made the Roc falter.

It was a Minotaur. But not the kind from old stories. This one was thirty feet tall, its skin crusted with stone, its horns wreathed in lightning. It carried a chain that dragged behind it, each link the size of a man's torso, and its eyes burned with red fire.

"Kill it," the announcer said, "and the winner of this round advances directly to the finals. Fail, and you're disqualified. All of you."

The tamers shouted. Curses. Protests. The scarred man laughed.

Su Yun looked down at the cub. It had climbed onto his shoulder, its tiny claws gripping his collar, and it was staring at the Minotaur with an expression he hadn't seen before.

Hunger.

"Little one," he whispered. "Don't—"

The cub leaped.

It flew through the air, a black streak against the firelight, and landed on the Minotaur's face. The beast roared, shaking its head, but the cub held on. Its claws dug into the stone skin. Its teeth sank into the Minotaur's eye.

And it began to feed.

Light poured from the Minotaur—white, gold, red—streaming into the cub's mouth like water into a drain. The beast's roars became screams. Its body shriveled, the stone cracking, the lightning fading. In ten seconds, it was a husk. In fifteen, it was dust.

The cub landed on the ground. It was bigger now. The size of a large dog. Its wings had grown, stretching wide, and its eyes—those black, depthless eyes—glowed with blue fire.

It turned to look at Su Yun.

And for the first time, he saw something in its gaze that terrified him.

Recognition.

*You're mine,* a voice whispered inside his skull. *And I am yours. Feed me, tamer. Feed me, or I'll feed on you.*

The bronze bell in his pocket grew cold.

The registrar's words echoed in his mind: *The Qiongqi is not a beast. It's a symptom. A crack in the fabric of the Nine Realms.*

But as the cub padded toward him, its tail swishing, its eyes bright with ancient hunger, Su Yun realized the truth.

The crack wasn't the Qiongqi.

The crack was him.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Beast That Eats Shadows

The cub's shadow stretched across the cave wall, and Su Yun saw it.

He saw the thing that lived in the shadow. The thing with no shape, no form, no name. He saw its eyes—if they could be called eyes—open in the darkness, and he felt something cold slide down his spine like a finger tracing his vertebrae.

"Don't look at it," the registrar said.

Su Yun couldn't look away.

The cub purred against his leg, oblivious. Innocent. The shadow-thing watched him with hunger that predated hunger, with want that existed before wanting was invented. It didn't move. It didn't need to. It was already everywhere the light wasn't.

"Look at me, boy."

The registrar grabbed his chin and forced his head around. Her fingers were iron, her eyes harder. "You looked. That's a problem. But it's not the biggest problem."

"What is that thing?"

"Your cub's shadow." She released him. "Or rather, what lives in it. The Qiongqi is a door, Su Yun. A door to places that were sealed for good reason. The cub doesn't know it yet. But the shadow does."

The cub stopped purring. It looked up at the registrar, and for a moment—just a moment—its eyes weren't black. They were the same nothing-space as the shadow. Then they flickered back to normal, and the cub yawned, showing tiny fangs.

Su Yun picked it up. It weighed nothing. It weighed everything.

"You said every hundred thousand years, a Qiongqi bonds with a tamer," he said. "What happened to the last one?"

The registrar was silent for a long time. The lantern flickered. The shadow-thing pressed against the edges of the light, testing, waiting.

"The last tamer was called Meng Xi," she finally said. "He was the greatest beast tamer in the history of the Nine Realms. He could command beasts with a whisper. He could make Xiezhi kneel. He could make dragons weep."

"What happened to him?"

"He consumed the Eastern Sea." Her voice was flat. "He consumed it completely. Every beast, every fish, every drop of water. He consumed it and the Qiongqi consumed him, and where the Eastern Sea had been, there was nothing. Just a hole in the world. A hole that's still there."

The cub yawned again. It didn't care about history. It didn't care about holes in the world. It cared about warmth and milk and the steady beat of Su Yun's heart.

"I won't let that happen," Su Yun said.

"You won't have a choice." The registrar walked to the back of the cave, where a stone door stood half-open. "The Qiongqi will grow. It will need to eat. And when it's hungry enough, it will eat through you."

"Then I'll starve it."

"You can't starve a Qiongqi. You can only delay it." She pushed the stone door open, revealing a narrow passage that smelled of wet earth and old blood. "This tunnel leads to the tournament's third stage. If you survive it, you'll be one step closer to saving your village."

"And if I don't?"

"Then your village dies. The Qiongqi finds another tamer. And the cycle continues." She stepped aside. "Your choice, boy. The tunnel or the crater."

The crater was still filled with chaos. Through the cave entrance, Su Yun could hear screams, beast roars, the crack of breaking stone. The Xiezhi was still out there. The scarred man. Everyone who wanted his cub dead.

The tunnel was dark. But the shadow-thing was already there.

"You're sending me to die," Su Yun said.

"I'm sending you to choose." The registrar's eyes were ancient, tired, full of things she'd seen that no one should see. "The tournament isn't about capturing beasts. It's about surviving long enough to become something worth surviving for. Your cub will eat everything you love. The only question is whether you'll have anything left by the time it's done."

The cub squirmed in Su Yun's arms. It wanted down. It wanted to walk.

He let it.

The cub padded to the tunnel entrance, sniffed the air, and looked back at him. Its tail flicked. Its ears perked. It was waiting.

"Fine," Su Yun said. "But if I die, I'm taking you with me."

The registrar almost smiled. "That's the spirit."

The tunnel went down.

Su Yun walked for what felt like hours, the cub padding beside him, the darkness pressing in from all sides. The only light came from patches of glowing moss that grew on the walls, casting everything in sickly green.

The cub stopped.

It sniffed the air. Its ears flattened against its skull. It growled—a low, rumbling sound that shouldn't have come from something so small.

"What is it?"

The cub didn't answer. It never answered. But it moved closer to Su Yun's legs, pressing against him, and that was answer enough.

Something was ahead.

Su Yun crouched, put his hand on the cub's back. "Stay close."

They moved forward together, slow, silent. The tunnel curved, then opened into a chamber the size of a small lake. The ceiling was lost in darkness. The floor was covered in bones—thousands of them, some beast, some human, all cracked and picked clean.

In the center of the chamber, something sat.

It was vaguely human-shaped, but its proportions were wrong. Its arms were too long. Its legs were too short. Its skin was the color of wet stone, and its face had no features—no eyes, no nose, no mouth—just a smooth, blank surface that somehow looked at Su Yun.

The cub froze.

The faceless thing tilted its head. A sound came from it—not a voice, but something like rocks grinding together, like bones breaking, like the earth remembering how to scream.

"You have the beast," it said.

The words didn't come from its face. They came from everywhere, from the walls, from the bones, from the darkness above. Su Yun's ears popped. His teeth ached.

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"The beast that shouldn't be." The faceless thing took a step forward. Its feet didn't break the bones. It walked on top of them, leaving no prints. "The beast that consumes. The beast that ends."

The cub growled again. Its fur bristled. Its tiny claws scraped the stone floor.

"I'm not here to fight you," Su Yun said. "I'm here to pass through."

"There is no passing through." The faceless thing stopped a few feet away. "There is only feeding. The tournament is a lie. The Nine Realms are a cage. And you—" It pointed at Su Yun with a finger that was too long, too thin, too wrong. "—are the key that opens the door."

Su Yun's hand went to the rope at his belt. It was a stupid weapon. A useless weapon. But it was all he had.

"I don't know what you are," he said. "But I know what I am. I'm a stable boy from a village that's going to die if I don't win this tournament. I'm not a key. I'm not a door. I'm just a kid with a cub and a rope."

The faceless thing was silent for a long moment.

Then it laughed.

The sound was worse than its voice. It was wet, broken, wrong. It came from the bones. It came from the walls. It came from the cub's shadow, where the thing with no shape watched and smiled.

"You are already consumed," the faceless thing said. "You just don't know it yet."

The cub lunged.

It moved faster than Su Yun had ever seen it move—a blur of black fur and tiny claws, straight at the faceless thing's throat. The faceless thing caught it mid-air, one hand wrapped around the cub's body, and held it up.

The cub screamed.

The same scream as before. The scream that cracked the ground, that made the Xiezhi step back. The scream of chaos made flesh.

But the faceless thing didn't step back.

It held the cub in front of its blank face, and something moved behind that smooth surface. Something that wanted.

"The Qiongqi is not your weapon," it said. "You are its vessel. And vessels are meant to be emptied."

The cub's eyes changed. They went from black to nothing. The shadow on the floor grew, spread, reached for Su Yun's feet.

"Let it go," Su Yun said.

"Or what?"

Su Yun didn't answer. He grabbed the rope from his belt, looped it around his hand, and swung.

The rope caught the faceless thing's wrist. Su Yun pulled, hard, and the faceless thing stumbled—not because the rope was strong, but because it hadn't expected the attack. The cub fell, hit the ground, and scrambled back to Su Yun.

The faceless thing looked at the rope around its wrist.

"You bind me with string?"

"I bind you with faith," Su Yun said. He didn't know where the words came from. They just came. "The rope is just rope. But the faith is mine."

The faceless thing's wrist began to smoke.

It looked down. The rope was burning—not with fire, but with light. White light. The kind of light that existed before the sun, before the moon, before the stars.

"Impossible," the faceless thing said.

"Nothing's impossible." Su Yun pulled the rope again. "I'm a stable boy. I don't know cultivation. I don't know beast taming. But I know one thing: you can't eat what you can't catch."

The faceless thing screamed.

The sound was worse than the cub's. It was the sound of something old and terrible being hurt by something small and foolish. The bones on the floor rattled. The walls cracked. The light from Su Yun's rope blazed brighter, hotter, until the faceless thing's entire arm was burning.

It tore itself free.

The arm came off at the shoulder, smoking, melting, dissolving into ash. The faceless thing staggered backward, its blank face somehow expressing agony.

"This isn't over," it said. "The tournament will end. The realms will fall. And you, stable boy—you will watch."

It dissolved into the darkness, leaving nothing but the smell of burnt stone and the echo of its promise.

Su Yun fell to his knees.

The cub pressed against him, trembling. The rope in his hands was just rope again—ordinary, frayed, useless.

But it had worked.

He had fought something that shouldn't exist. And he had won.

"I don't understand," he whispered.

The cub looked up at him. Its eyes were normal again—black, wide, innocent. It licked his chin.

Behind them, in the tunnel, footsteps echoed.

Su Yun turned. A figure emerged from the darkness—tall, cloaked, carrying a lantern that burned with blue fire.

"Well, well," the figure said. "You survived the Hollow Man."

The voice was familiar.

The figure lowered its hood.

It was the scarred man from the crater. The one whose Xiezhi had nearly killed them.

"You," Su Yun said.

"Me." The scarred man smiled. It was not a kind smile. "I've been following you since the cave. I wanted to see if you'd die."

"Sorry to disappoint."

"You didn't disappoint." The scarred man walked closer, his lantern casting long shadows. "You did something no one has done in a thousand years. You hurt a Hollow Man with nothing but a rope."
